segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, illuminated by a single spotlight on a dark, minimalist stage, confidently addresses a large, dimly lit audience. Dressed in his signature black leather jacket, glasses, and headset microphone, he gestures expressively while holding a remote, explaining how a single inference application interacting with a chatbot requires a supercomputer to run. He emphasizes that 'the future is generative with these chatbots,' which involve 'trillions of tokens, trillions of parameters,' needing to generate tokens at 'interactive rates.' As he speaks, a large technical diagram behind him displays a stacked server rack, detailing 'Train GPT-MoE-1.8T in 90 Days' with 'Blackwell GB200 NVL72, 2000 GPUs | 4MW, 1/4th the Power,' featuring the NVIDIA logo. Huang humorously clarifies that 'three tokens is about a word,' then recites 'space, the final frontier' as an example of '80 tokens,' eliciting an implied amused reaction from the audience as he self-deprecatingly remarks, 'I don't know if that's useful to you,' and later, 'this is not going well,' highlighting the challenge of good analogies in communication. The on-screen text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo remain visible throughout this high-tech presentation.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt and wearing glasses, stands center stage against a dark, minimalist backdrop, illuminated by focused lighting. He holds a small black remote and speaks with a clear, engaging voice, using expressive hand gestures that frequently spread wide or come together to emphasize his points. He begins with a lighthearted remark about not knowing what 'he's talking about' and never having 'seen Star Trek,' before transitioning to explain the technical challenge of 'generating these tokens.' He emphasizes that when interacting, 'you're hoping that the tokens come back to you as quickly as possible, and as quickly as you could read it,' stressing the importance of the 'ability for generation tokens.' Huang details the necessity to 'parallelize the work of this model across many, many GPUs' to achieve 'throughput,' which 'reduces the cost, the overall cost per token of generating,' and dictates 'the cost of delivering the service.' He then introduces 'another interactive rate, which is another tokens per second, where it's about per user,' linking it to 'quality of service.' He concludes by stating these two aspects 'compete against each other' and that they 'have to find a way to distribute work across all of these different GPUs and parallelize it in a way that allows us to achieve both.' Throughout his dynamic presentation, the camera maintains a medium shot, occasionally zooming out to reveal the dimly lit audience seated in rows, while 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo are visible on-screen, reinforcing the professional and energetic keynote atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark stage, holding a small black remote. He gestures towards a large screen displaying a 'GPT-MoE 1.8T Inference' performance graph, dated 'MARCH 18, 2024'. Huang explains the graph, noting the 'enormous' search space and acknowledging audience 'gasps' at the complexity. He clarifies the Y-axis as 'tokens per second, data center throughput' and the X-axis as 'tokens per second, interactivity of the person', emphasizing that the 'upper right is the best' for optimal performance. He highlights the superior green line labeled 'GB200 FP4', which traces an optimal curve across various configurations like 'TP2.EP8.DP4' and 'TP4.EP16'. Huang explains that the blue data points on the graph resulted from 'some repartitioning of the software' and that an 'optimizing solution has to go and figure out whether to use tensor' parallelization, referencing the 'Multi-Dimensional Optimization' box. He continues to move and gesture, explaining the intricate balance of performance metrics, with the 'y/ highlights' logo visible in the bottom right corner.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang standing on a dark stage, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote. He gestures toward a large screen displaying a performance graph titled 'GPT-MoE 1.8T Inference (seqlen=32k/1k, FTL=5s)'. The graph plots 'Throughput per GPU Tokens per Second' on the y-axis against 'Interactivity per User Tokens per Second' on the x-axis, with blue data points forming a dense cluster and a green line labeled 'GB200 FP4' tracing an optimal performance curve. The graph highlights three key configurations: 'TP2.EP8.DP4', 'TP2.EP16.PP2', and 'TP4.EP16', with the green line indicating the superior performance of the GB200 FP4 setup. In the top right corner, a box lists 'Multi-Dimensional Optimization: Tensor Parallel, Pipeline Parallel, Expert Parallel, Data Parallel'. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are visible in the top right and bottom right corners, respectively. Jensen Huang moves across the stage, pointing to different parts of the graph, emphasizing the trade-off between throughput and interactivity, and explaining how the GB200 system achieves high performance across various parallelization strategies. The lighting remains focused on him and the screen, maintaining a high-contrast, professional presentation atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, dynamically gesturing with both hands and holding a small black remote. Behind him, a large screen displays a performance graph titled 'GPT-MoE 1.8T Inference (seqlen=32k/1k, FTL=5s)', plotting 'Throughput per GPU Tokens per Second' against 'Interactivity per User Tokens per Second', featuring blue data points and a green line labeled 'GB200 FP4'. Huang speaks clearly and emphatically, explaining the 'Multi-Dimensional Optimization' strategies listed on the screen, such as 'tensor parallel, expert parallel, pipeline parallel, or data parallel', which are used to "distribute this enormous model across all these different GPUs and sustain the performance that you need." He emphasizes that this "exploration space would be impossible if not for the programmability of NVIDIA's GPUs," crediting CUDA and their rich ecosystem for enabling them to "explore this universe and find that green roofline." He points towards the screen, detailing the green line and its associated configurations, explaining, "you got TP2, EP8, DP4. It means two parallel, two uh tensor parallel, tensor parallel across two GPUs, expert parallel across eight, data parallel across four." He further notes, "on the other end, you got tensor parallel across four and expert parallel across 16," highlighting how "the configuration, the distribution of that software, it's a different, different uh runtime that would produce these different results." The camera alternates between wide shots showing the full graph and close-ups of Huang, with 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo visible on screen, all under focused lighting that maintains a professional and technical atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark stage under focused lighting, holding a small black remote. He gestures expressively, occasionally adjusting his glasses and touching his ear, as he explains that the displayed concept is just one model and one computer configuration. He emphasizes the vast number of models and system configurations becoming available, stating, "In one generation, because we created a system that's designed for trillion parameter generative AI, the inference capability of Blackwell is off the charts." He then proudly declares, "And in fact, it is some 30 times Hopper," as the audience applauds. The scene transitions to a static technical performance graph titled 'GPT-MoE 1.8T Inference (seqlen=32k/1k, FTL=5s)', which plots 'Throughput per GPU Tokens per Second' against 'Interactivity per User Tokens per Second'. The graph visually supports his claim, showing a dominant green line labeled 'GB200 FP4' (Blackwell 30X Hopper) significantly outperforming the red (B200 FP8) and blue (H200 FP8) lines. Huang continues, explaining that for large language models like ChatGPT, "the blue line is Hopper," and that they achieved this leap not by changing Hopper's architecture but by making it a "bigger chip" and utilizing advanced connectivity for higher data transfer rates. The graph also details parallelization configurations like 'TP2.EP8.DP4' and highlights 'Multi-Dimensional Optimization'. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' is visible in the top right, with a purple 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a detailed technical graph on a black background, titled 'GPT-MoE 1.8T Inference (seqlen=32k/1k, FTL=5s)', plotting 'Throughput per GPU Tokens per Second' against 'Interactivity per User Tokens per Second'. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, wearing a black leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, then appears on a dark stage, holding a small black remote. He gestures expressively as he explains how a 'giant 208 billion parameter chip' would perform, referring to a 'purple line' on the graph as 'quite wonderfully' but not as great as it could be. Huang highlights that the NVLink switch communicates 'almost 10 times faster' than previous networks, making Blackwell an 'amazing system for generative AI'. He concludes by stating that in the future, data centers will be thought of as 'an AI factory', whose 'goal in life' is to produce AI. The 'MARCH 18, 2024' date and 'y/ highlights' logo remain visible throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket and white sneakers, standing center stage under a focused spotlight. Behind him, a massive screen displays a grid of logos from major tech companies like AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, Meta, OpenAI, and NVIDIA, with 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo visible. Jensen Huang gestures expressively with both hands, moving slightly as he speaks with an enthusiastic tone, declaring "Unbelievable excitement for Blackwell! Unbelievable excitement." He details "a whole bunch of different configurations," including those that "slide into the Hopper form factor" for easy upgrades, and "liquid-cooled" extreme versions, mentioning a "one entire rack that's connected by NVLink 72." The camera zooms in for a medium close-up, capturing his animated delivery as he explains that Blackwell is "ramping to the world's AI companies," "CSPs," "OEMs and ODMs," "regional clouds," "sovereign AIs," and "Telcos all over the world are signing up to launch with Blackwell." As he finishes this statement, the audience erupts in applause, which is then visually confirmed by a wide-angle shot revealing the vast, dimly lit auditorium filled with an attentive crowd, bathed in purple accent lighting, creating an atmosphere of high-tech grandeur and anticipation.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, holding a small black remote. He gestures expressively as he speaks, declaring Blackwell will be the most successful product launch in NVIDIA's history. On-screen text in the top right corner reads 'MARCH 18, 2024', with a purple 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right. A large screen behind him displays a detailed slide titled 'AWS and NVIDIA expand joint AI offerings', highlighting collaborations such as AWS AI Security, Project Ceiba, and integrations with Amazon SageMaker and NVIDIA NIM. The slide also features logos for AWS and NVIDIA, along with technical details like 'NVIDIA BLACKWELL COMING TO AWS' and '414 EXAFLOPS | 20,736 GB200 | GEN 4 AWS EFA'. Jensen Huang elaborates on the AWS collaboration, mentioning they are building the first GPU with secure AI and a 222 exaflops system, referring to a previous animation of a digital twin representing the scale of their work. As the screen transitions to a simpler view showing only the AWS and NVIDIA logos side by side, he continues to detail their extensive partnership, highlighting CUDA acceleration for SageMaker AI and Bedrock AI, Amazon Robotics' use of NVIDIA Omniverse and Isaac Sim, and NVIDIA Health integration into AWS Health, emphasizing AWS's commitment to accelerated computing.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens on March 18, 2024, with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote, standing on a dark, minimalist stage under focused lighting. A purple 'y/ highlights' logo is visible in the bottom right. Behind him, a large screen initially displays a Google Cloud and NVIDIA collaboration slide, detailing AI platforms, accelerated computing, and tools like Vertex AI, TensorRT, and Blackwell. Jensen Huang gestures expressively as he speaks, stating, "Google is gearing up for Blackwell. GCP already has A100s, H100s, T4s, L4s, a whole fleet of NVIDIA CUDA GPUs, and they recently announced the Gemma model that runs across all of it." He continues, explaining, "We're working to optimize and accelerate every aspect of GCP. We're accelerating data proc with for data processing, their data processing engine, Jax, XLA, Vertex AI, and Mujoco for robotics. So we're working with Google and GCP across a whole bunch of initiatives." The screen then transitions to a black background with Google and NVIDIA logos, followed by an Oracle and NVIDIA collaboration slide. Jensen Huang continues, "Oracle is gearing up for Blackwell. Oracle is a great partner of ours for NVIDIA DGX Cloud, and we're also working together to accelerate something that's really important to a lot of companies, Oracle database." He then moves on as the screen shows a Microsoft and NVIDIA slide detailing AI innovations, Azure integrations, and NVIDIA H100 NVL, stating, "Microsoft is accelerating and Microsoft is gearing up for Blackwell. Microsoft and NVIDIA has a wide-ranging partnership. We're accelerating CUDA, accelerating all kinds of services when you when you chat obviously and AI services that are in Microsoft Azure." Throughout, he maintains an engaging and dynamic presence, turning to face different parts of the audience, highlighting the professional, high-tech atmosphere focused on strategic AI and cloud computing partnerships.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, wearing a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, actively gesturing on a dark stage. He introduces the concept, stating, "Creating a digital twin to build a computer that was built in a digital twin. And so, let me show you what Wistron's doing." The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are displayed. An upbeat, corporate background music begins as the scene transitions to close-ups of NVIDIA-branded server racks, followed by the Wistron logo on a building. A female narrator explains that "To meet the demand for NVIDIA accelerated computing, Wistron, one of our leading manufacturing partners, is building digital twins of NVIDIA DGX and HGX factories using custom software developed with Omniverse SDKs and APIs." The video then showcases a highly detailed 'Omniverse Digital Twin' of a factory floor, featuring rows of white machinery, conveyor belts, and real-time status indicators. The narrator adds that "For their newest factory, Wistron started with a digital twin to virtually integrate their multi-CAD and process simulation data into a unified view." A technical diagram illustrates the integration of various software tools (3ds Max, Ansys, etc.) with NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud via USD APIs and Omniverse Channel APIs, aligning with the narrator's mention of SDKs and APIs. The digital twin is shown from multiple angles, emphasizing its scale and complexity. The narrator highlights that "Testing and optimizing layouts in this physically accurate digital environment increased worker efficiency by 51%." The scene cuts to a real-world, unfinished industrial space with exposed pipes and concrete floors, then transitions back to the digital twin overlaid on the physical space, demonstrating augmented reality applications. The narrator further explains, "During construction, the Omniverse Digital Twin was used to verify that the physical build matched the digital plans. Identifying any discrepancies early has helped avoid costly change orders." A person is shown using a tablet to view the digital twin in the real environment, followed by a woman wearing an Apple Vision Pro headset, walking through the space and interacting with the virtual overlay. The video concludes with a close-up of a conveyor system within the digital twin, highlighting the integration of physical and virtual manufacturing environments, as the narrator states, "And the results have been impressive. Using a digital twin has..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a detailed 3D simulation of an Omniverse Digital Twin, showcasing a highly automated industrial facility. White machinery, conveyor belts, and real-time status indicators like 'MOUNTER' and 'PRESSFIT' with UPH metrics are visible. The camera glides through rows of equipment, displaying green, yellow, and red status lights indicating cycle times relative to standard performance. An NVIDIA-branded component is highlighted within a server rack, emphasizing the technology's integration. A female voice explains how the Omniverse Digital Twin helped bring Wistron's factory online in half the time, just two and a half months, and how it aids Wistron in rapidly testing new layouts, improving operations, and monitoring real-time activities using live IoT data. This ultimately enabled Wistron to reduce end-to-end cycle times by 50% and defect rates by 40%. The voice continues, stating that with NVIDIA AI and Omniverse, NVIDIA's global ecosystem of partners is building a new era of accelerated, AI-enabled digitalization. The scene then transitions to a real-world commemorative photo of the Wistron N2 Factory's inaugural batch shipment on March 8th, displayed on a large screen to a live audience seated in a dimly lit auditorium with purple and blue lighting. The audience applauds as NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, wearing a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt and black pants, takes the minimalist, dark stage. Holding a small black remote, he gestures as he speaks, his voice clear and enthusiastic, declaring, "That's how we... That's the way it's going to be in the future, we're manufacturing everything digitally first, and then we'll manufacture it physically." He then asks, "People ask me, how did it start? What got you guys so excited?" Throughout the segment, 'MARCH 18, 2024' is displayed in the top right corner, and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, holding a small remote and gesturing animatedly. The large screen behind him initially displays a 3D industrial facility with 'i am a transformer' text, along with the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo. Huang speaks passionately, asking, "What was it that you saw that caused you to put it all in on this incredible idea?" As he walks across the stage, emphasizing his points with expansive hand movements, the screen transitions to a diagram illustrating the 2012 AlexNet 'First Contact' with AI vision, showing a kitten photo leading to a neural network schematic and then to a 'CAT' label. Huang explains, "This, as you know, was first contact. 2012, AlexNet. You put a cat into this computer, and it comes out and it says, 'Cat'." He continues, his voice rising with excitement, "And we said, 'Oh my god, this is gonna change everything'." The camera zooms in on him as he elaborates on the technical process, describing how "1 million numbers across three channels, RGB... make no sense to anybody," but when put into software, it "dimensionally reduce it... from a million dimensions... It turns it into three letters. One vector." His expressions and hand motions underscore the transformative impact of this AI breakthrough, all within the high-contrast, professional atmosphere of the tech keynote.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage under focused lighting, holding a small black remote. Behind him, a large screen displays an AI Vision Diagram, illustrating the foundational moment of AI image recognition with a kitten photo, an arrow to the 2012 AlexNet neural network labeled 'FIRST CONTACT', and another arrow leading to a 'CAT' box. As the camera gradually zooms in on him from a wide shot to a medium close-up, Huang gestures expressively with both hands, speaking clearly and emphatically. He explains how the AI is "generalized," capable of recognizing "different cats," even the "front of the cat and the back of the cat," exclaiming "unbelievable!" He continues, highlighting that the AI "was able to recognize all these cats" and that they realized "how it did it, systematically, structurally, it's scalable." He poses the question, "How big can you make it? Well, how big do you want to make it?" Huang then declares, "this is a completely new way of writing software," and demonstrates, "you type in the word C-A-T. And what comes out is a cat! It went the other way. Am I right? Unbelievable!" The 'MARCH 18, 2024' text remains in the top right corner, and the 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right, throughout his animated and professional presentation.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, holding a small black remote. He gestures expressively with both hands as he speaks, asking, "How is it possible?" and explaining how AI can take "three letters" and generate "a million pixels" that make sense, calling it "the miracle." He notes that just ten years later, AI can recognize and understand not only text, images, videos, and sounds but also their meaning, allowing it to chat and summarize. As the camera pulls back, a massive screen behind him is revealed, displaying a complex diagram titled 'LEARN AND UNDERSTAND EVERYTHING'. This diagram features a central AI model with multiple inputs like TEXT, IMAGE, VIDEO, SPEECH, MULTI-MODAL, AMINO ACID, and BRAINWAVES, connected to outputs such as IMAGE, VIDEO, 3D, SOUND, ANIMATION, MANIPULATION, PROTEIN, and SPEECH, complete with visual examples. Jensen walks across the stage, gesturing towards the screen, emphasizing that AI doesn't just recognize pixels but "understood the pixels" and can even "condition it between two modalities," like language conditioning an image to generate "all kinds of interesting things." He then poses the question, "What else can you understand that you've digitized?" to the attentively watching, dimly lit audience, maintaining a futuristic, tech-focused atmosphere. White text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo are visible in the bottom right.

On the right, outputs are shown: IMAGE, VIDEO, 3D, SOUND, ANIMATION, MANIPULATION, PROTEIN, and SPEECH, with corresponding visual examples like a cat image, a 3D model, and a robot. segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, in his distinctive black textured leather jacket, black shirt, pants, and white sneakers, stands under a spotlight on a dark stage, holding a remote and gesturing expressively. His gray hair and glasses are clearly visible as he articulates, "text and, you know, images is because we digitize those, but what else have we digitized? Well, it turns out we digitize a lot of things. Proteins and genes and brainwaves. Anything you can digitize, so long as there's structure, we can probably learn some patterns from it, and if we can learn the patterns from it, we can understand its meaning. If we can understand its meaning, we might be able to generate it as well. And so therefore, the generative AI revolution is here." As he speaks, the camera slowly zooms out to reveal a vast, dimly lit auditorium filled with an audience, while a massive, rotating digital Earth, displaying continents, cloud patterns, and atmospheric effects, appears on a large screen to his left. This visual underscores his subsequent point: "Well, what else can we generate, what else can we learn? Well, one of the things that we would love to learn... is we would love to learn climate. We would love to learn extreme weather. We would love to learn how we can predict future weather at regional scales, at sufficiently high resolution, such that we can keep people out of harm's way before harm comes." The on-screen text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo brand the grand, futuristic presentation, as he concludes by mentioning, "Extreme weather cost the world 150 billion..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a serene, full-globe view of Earth from space, centered on the Indian and Pacific Oceans, displaying visible landmasses and swirling cloud patterns, with 'MARCH 18, 2024' in the top right and the 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right. A male speaker discusses the need to adapt and introduces "Earth 2," a digital twin of the Earth for predicting weather, and "CorrDiff," an extraordinary invention using generative AI for high-resolution weather prediction. As he says "Let's take a look," dramatic, suspenseful music begins. The view then rapidly zooms into Taiwan, transitioning to a split-screen montage of storm footage, including crashing waves, heavy rain, and wind-battered infrastructure, accompanied by WION news tickers reporting 'TYPHOON CHANTHU DRENCHES TAIWAN' and 'OVER 100 FLIGHTS CANCELLED'. A female speaker explains that as Earth's climate changes, AI-powered forecasting helps track severe storms like Super Typhoon Chanthu, which caused damage in Taiwan in 2021. She notes that current AI forecast models, while accurate in tracking, are limited to 25 km resolution, which can miss important details. Visually, this is shown as a 'Typical AI Forecast 25 km resolution' with a broad, low-resolution heat map of the typhoon. The female speaker then introduces "NVIDIA's CorrDiff" as a revolutionary new generative AI model, coinciding with the visual transition to a 'CorrDiff 2 km resolution' view, displaying a dramatically more detailed, high-resolution, swirling storm system in vivid purples and oranges, highlighting its enhanced precision.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a high-resolution satellite visualization of a storm system over Taiwan, labeled 'CorrDiff 2 km resolution,' swirling in vivid purples and oranges, as a female speaker explains that CorrDiff uses high-resolution radar-assimilated weather forecasts and reanalysis data to super-resolve extreme events like Typhoon Chanthu from 25 km to 2 km resolution, achieving 1000 times the speed and 3000 times the energy efficiency of conventional models. The scene transitions to a split-screen comparison, with the left showing a 'Global AI Forecast 25 km resolution' of Taiwan, less detailed, while the right displays the intense, fiery storm patterns of CorrDiff, emphasizing the resolution contrast. The speaker continues, detailing how combining NVIDIA's FourCastNet and generative AI models like CorrDiff allows for exploring hundreds or thousands of kilometer-scale regional weather forecasts to predict the best, worst, and most likely impacts of a storm, which can minimize loss of life and property. A global perspective then appears, featuring a grid of smaller storm simulations surrounding a central map of East Asia, labeled 'NVIDIA FourCastNet and CorrDiff Ensemble of Typhoon Track Predictions,' with multiple yellow and white predictive tracks fanning out from a storm near Taiwan. The camera zooms out to a full Earth view, highlighting the storm region with a white box, as the speaker notes that CorrDiff is currently optimized for Taiwan, but generative supersampling will soon be available globally as part of the NVIDIA Earth-2 inference service. The scene then transitions to a live audience in a large auditorium, bathed in purple lighting, attentively watching the presentation, concluding with their applause. Throughout, the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo remain visible.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, dynamically gesturing with a small black remote as he speaks. On-screen text in the top right corner reads 'MARCH 18, 2024', with a purple 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right. He begins by discussing 'The Weather Company', whose logo appears alongside NVIDIA's on the screen behind him, identifying it as 'the trusted source of global weather predictions'. Huang explains their collaboration to 'accelerate their weather simulation' and integrate 'Earth-2 Co-Dev' for 'regional, high-resolution weather prediction', calling it 'really exciting work'. He then transitions to a slide titled 'NVIDIA HEALTHCARE', showcasing four key areas: IMAGING & ROBOTICS (with MONAI | Holoscan), GENOMICS (with Parabricks), BIOINFORMATICS (with RAPIDS and pandas), and DRUG DISCOVERY (with BioNeMo), each with vivid 3D visualizations. Huang proudly states that NVIDIA Healthcare was started '15 years ago' and is an area they are 'very, very proud' of, covering 'medical imaging', 'gene sequencing', and 'computational chemistry', with NVIDIA being the 'computation behind it'. He announces they will do 'something really, really cool' involving 'all of these AI models'. Throughout, the camera alternates between wide shots of the stage and closer views of Jensen Huang, maintaining focus on his presentation and the evolving on-screen content.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, holding a small black remote. He gestures expressively and shifts his stance as he articulately explains how NVIDIA's work extends beyond generating images and audio, focusing on the "digitization that we've done for genes and proteins and amino acids." He states that this capability is now "passed through machine learning so that we understand the language of life." At 0:06, a large screen behind him displays the 'NVIDIA HEALTHCARE' slide, which is divided into four sections: IMAGING & ROBOTICS, GENOMICS, BIOINFORMATICS, and DRUG DISCOVERY, each featuring 3D visualizations and associated tools like MONAI | Holoscan, Parabricks, RAPIDS, pandas, and BioNeMo. Jensen Huang walks slightly to the center, gesturing toward the screen as he continues to speak about the "ability to understand the language of life," referencing the "first evidence of it with AlphaFold." He describes AlphaFold as an "extraordinary thing," noting that after "decades of painstaking work," the world had only digitized and reconstructed 200,000 proteins using techniques like cryo-electron microscopy or X-ray crystallography. He then powerfully contrasts this by stating that "in just less than a year or so, AlphaFold has reconstructed 200 million proteins," essentially "every protein." The on-screen text 'MARCH 18, 2024' is displayed in the top right, and the 'y/ highlights' logo appears in the bottom right, while Huang returns to his central position, his speech directly reinforcing the themes of genomics and drug discovery presented on the slide.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, a man with gray hair wearing a black textured leather jacket, black pants, and white sneakers, standing alone on a dark, minimalist stage illuminated by a single spotlight. He gestures expressively, holding a small black remote, as he speaks about revolutionary models for every living thing that's ever been sequenced, noting their difficulty to build and NVIDIA's commitment to building them for researchers worldwide. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are displayed on screen. The scene transitions to a presentation slide titled 'NVIDIA HEALTHCARE', showcasing four domains: IMAGING & ROBOTICS, GENOMICS, BIOINFORMATICS, and DRUG DISCOVERY, each with corresponding 3D visualizations and tools like MONAI | Holoscan, Parabricks, RAPIDS, pandas, and BioNeMo. Following this, a dynamic 3D animation of colorful molecular structures appears, succeeded by a visualization of a protein structure with a molecule binding to it. An upbeat, slightly futuristic instrumental background music begins as a female narrator explains that virtual screening for new medicines is computationally intractable, with existing techniques requiring days to scan billions of compounds. She then introduces NVIDIA BioNeMo NIMS as enabling a new generative screening paradigm, which is visually represented by a technical flowchart illustrating a drug discovery pipeline. This flowchart shows a user input branching to AlphaFold2 and MolMIM, then integrating with UniDock and DiffDock, and finally outputting optimized molecules, with a 'Gradient-Free Optimization' step added mid-flow. The narrator specifies that NIMS uses AlphaFold for protein structure prediction, MolMIM for molecule generation, and DiffDock for docking, allowing candidate molecules to be generated and screened in minutes. The entire sequence maintains a dark, high-contrast aesthetic with focused lighting and digital graphics, emphasizing the technological and scientific themes.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a technical diagram, dated 'MARCH 18, 2024' with the 'y/ highlights' logo, illustrating a drug discovery pipeline integrating tools like AlphaFold2, MolMIM, UniDock, and DiffDock, with a 'Gradient-Free Optimization' step highlighted. A female speaker explains how this connects to "custom applications to steer the generative process, iteratively optimizing for desired properties," noting these applications can be defined with "BioNeMo microservices or built from scratch." This transitions to a 3D visualization of evolving molecular structures on a grid, analyzed via 2D and 3D scatter plots with axes for LogP, Molecular Weight, and QED, as the speaker describes a "physics-based simulation" optimizing for a "molecule's ability to bind to a target protein" and other "favorable molecular properties in parallel." Histograms then compare 'reference' and 'generated' distributions for Docking Score, QED, and LogP, followed by four molecular docking simulations with chemical formulas and QED scores. The speaker highlights that "MolMIM generates high-quality, drug-like molecules that bind to the target and are synthesizable," leading to faster development of successful medicines. The video shifts to the NVIDIA BioNeMo web interface, a cloud service for generative AI in drug discovery, showing a protein folding simulation with a 3D model and prediction score gradient. The speaker elaborates that "BioNeMo is enabling a new paradigm in drug discovery with NIMS, providing on-demand microservices that can be combined to build powerful drug discovery workflows like de novo protein design or guided molecule generation for virtual screening." A Jupyter notebook interface appears, displaying Python code for molecular visualization and a 3D protein-molecule interaction, followed by a 'Playground' interface where a user inputs a UniProt ID to generate a 3D protein structure. The segment concludes with a cinematic 3D animation of a molecule binding to a protein, shown in multiple split screens, and a wide shot of a large, dimly lit auditorium audience, bathed in purple light, watching the presentation on a large screen, as the speaker states "BioNeMo NIMS are helping researchers and developers reinvent computational drug design." Throughout, a consistent, subtle background music plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a dark stage where NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands before a large screen displaying a 3D molecular structure, with 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo visible. As initial audience applause fades, Jensen Huang begins speaking clearly and articulately about 'NVIDIA MoM' and a 'whole bunch of other models,' including computer vision, robotics, and 'terrific open-source language models.' The scene transitions to a closer view of him, holding a small black remote and gesturing expressively as the background shifts to a black screen. He emphasizes that while these models are 'groundbreaking,' companies face challenges in using, integrating, packaging, and running them, questioning, 'How would you do the optimization for each and every one of these models and put together the computing stack necessary to run that supercomputer so that you can run these models in your company?' The screen then displays a grid of company logos, including Google, Meta, Microsoft, and NVIDIA, among others, representing a collaborative AI ecosystem. Jensen Huang stands center stage, occasionally gesturing toward the static logos, concluding by announcing, 'we have a great idea. We're going to invent a new way... for you to receive and operate software,' all within a high-contrast, professional keynote atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, holding a small black remote. He gestures expressively while speaking with a clear, authoritative voice, introducing new software that "comes basically in a digital box" called a "container." The scene transitions to a full-screen graphic titled 'NVIDIA INFERENCE MICROSERVICE,' featuring a green hexagonal icon with interconnected nodes, while Huang names it "the NVIDIA Inference Microservice, a NIM." He explains that NIM is a "pre-trained model," "packaged and optimized to run across NVIDIA's installed base," which is "very, very large." The presentation then shifts to a detailed technical diagram illustrating the architecture of the inference microservice. Huang continues to elaborate, stating that "what's inside it is incredible," detailing how it includes "pre-trained, state-of-the-art, open-source models" and is "packaged up with all of its dependencies," such as "CUDA, the right version," "cuDNN, the right version," "TensorRT LLM, distributing across the multiple GPUs," and "Triton Inference Server," all "completely packaged together." He emphasizes that it's "optimized depending on whether you have a single GPU, multi-GPU, or multi-node of GPUs." The diagram, which remains on screen, shows a layered stack with 'NVIDIA CUDA' and 'Kubernetes' at the base, supporting the core microservice with 'Industry Standard APIs,' 'Triton Inference Server,' 'Cloud Native Stack,' 'Enterprise Management,' 'TensorRT LLM and Triton,' 'Optimized Model,' and 'Customization Cache,' all built upon '100's of Millions of CUDA GPUs Installed Base.' Throughout, the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are visible in the top right and bottom right corners, respectively.

The entire stack is anchored by the text '100's of Millions of CUDA GPUs Installed Base' at the bottom. The diagram remains on screen for the remainder of the segment, emphasizing the comprehensive, cloud-native, and enterprise-ready nature of the solution.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage under focused lighting, holding a small black remote. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo are displayed. Speaking with a clear, authoritative voice, he explains how "chatbots" will be integrated into "NIMs" and how "software is gonna be built someday" by assembling "a team of AIs." As he speaks, a large, complex technical diagram illustrating an AI agent architecture appears behind him, featuring a central orange 'Plan' block. Huang gestures expressively, detailing how a "Super AI" will take a given mission and break it down into an "execution plan." He moves across the stage, pointing to the diagram's left side, explaining how parts of this plan could be "handed off to another NIM" that understands systems like "SAP" or "Service Now" to "retrieve some information from their platforms," connecting to the 'Vector Database', 'Structured Data (ERP, CRM)', and 'Knowledge Graph' blocks. He then indicates the right side, describing how results might be passed to "another NIM" for "calculation," possibly an "optimization software" or "combinatorial optimization algorithm," linking to 'Other Agents' and 'Application Platform'. He occasionally steps back into a close-up shot to emphasize key points before returning to the wide view to continue his detailed explanation of the AI workflow.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a dark stage, where NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands holding a small black remote. Behind him, a large, illuminated technical diagram titled 'Technical Diagram on Screen' illustrates an AI agent system architecture, featuring a central 'Plan' block connected to various data sources and outputs, with 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo visible on screen. Jensen Huang speaks animatedly, explaining how an AI agent might use tools like 'Pandas' for numerical analysis, receive answers, and combine them, emphasizing that because it's been 'presented with this is what the right answer should look like,' it 'knows what right answers to produce.' As he gestures expressively, occasionally spreading his arms wide or pointing with the remote, the camera zooms in for a medium close-up, capturing his animated facial expressions. He continues, detailing how such a system could generate daily reports related to 'a bill plan or some forecast or some customer alert or some bugs database,' and how 'all these NIMs' (NVIDIA Inference Microservices) can assemble these reports. He highlights that these 'NIMs' are 'packaged up and ready to work on your systems,' provided there are 'NVIDIA GPUs in your data center in the cloud,' allowing them to 'work together as a team and do amazing things.' The scene transitions back to a wide shot as he walks slightly across the stage, concluding that NVIDIA has 'NIMs running all over the company' and is creating 'chatbots' with this technology.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, stands on a dark stage, gesturing with a small remote as he presents a technical diagram illustrating an AI agent system architecture. He speaks with a clear, engaging voice about the importance of "chip designer chatbots" and "AI co-pilots" that serve as "co-designers with our engineers." The screen transitions from a close-up of Huang to a prompt-response interface, displaying the question, 'What is CTL? Write an example CTL test.' Huang explains that they used a "Llama 2, 70B" model, which he refers to as "NVIDIA ChipNeMo Llama 2 70B Before Fine-Tuning" as seen on the slide, to answer this query. The interface shows a detailed response explaining Combinational Timing Logic (CTL) with a state machine test example. Huang notes that while the model described "conventional knowledge of CTL," the initial answer was "not very useful" and humorously states, "it's completely wrong," indicating the need for further fine-tuning and new examples.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a technical diagram on screen, showcasing a prompt-response system featuring NVIDIA ChipNeMo with Llama 2 70B, explicitly labeled 'With Fine-Tuning'. A prompt box asks, 'What is CTL? Write an example CTL test,' and a detailed explanation of CTL (Compute Trace Library) with a C++ code example appears as the response. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang's voice explains, "this is what a CTL is at NVIDIA," detailing that "CTL stands for Compute Trace Library" and marveling that "it wrote the program. Isn't that amazing?" This is followed by a wide shot of a large, dimly lit audience bathed in purple lighting, who respond with enthusiastic applause and cheering. The camera then focuses on Jensen Huang, who walks onto the dark, minimalist stage, wearing a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote. He gestures as he continues to speak, stating, "the productivity of our chip designers can go up. This is what you can do with a NIM." Behind him, a complex architectural diagram titled 'NeMo Microservices' is displayed, illustrating components like 'Foundation Model,' 'NeMo Curator Microservice,' 'NeMo Customizer Microservice,' 'NeMo Evaluator Microservice,' and 'NeMo Guardrails Microservice,' all feeding into a 'Custom Model in NIM,' with inputs labeled 'Prompt' and 'Event' and framed by a glowing green border. Jensen Huang elaborates on the diagram, explaining how the NeMo Microservice helps to "curate the data," "fine-tune them," "guardrail it," and "evaluate the answer." He then introduces "three elements, three pillars of what we're doing," starting with "inventing the technology." Throughout the segment, the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo remain visible in the top right and bottom right corners, respectively.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket and white sneakers, stands on a minimally lit dark stage, gesturing expressively with a small black remote as he presents. Behind him, a large screen displays a technical diagram titled 'NeMo Microservices', illustrating a system architecture with components like 'Foundation Model', 'NeMo Curator Microservice', 'NeMo Customizer Microservice', 'NeMo Evaluator Microservice', and 'NeMo Guardrails Microservice', all connected to a central 'Custom Model in NIM'. Jensen articulately explains NVIDIA's role in packaging and running AI models, stating, "The second is to create tools to help you modify it... First is having the AI technology, second is to help you modify it, and third is infrastructure for you to fine-tune it and if you like, deploy it." He emphasizes deployment flexibility, mentioning their "DGX Cloud" infrastructure or on-premise options, and declares NVIDIA an "AI foundry," comparing their role to TSMC's in chip manufacturing. He identifies the three pillars as "the NIMs, NeMo Microservice, and DGX Cloud." As he speaks, the diagram transitions to a new slide showing a 'Vector Database' with 'RAPIDS cuVS Accelerated Indexing' and 'Accelerated NN Search', connected to 'Embedding Microservices', 'Retrieval Microservices', and 'Text-to-SQL Microservice', all feeding into a central 'Plan' block. Jensen continues to walk and gesture, explaining that the system can be taught "to understand your proprietary information," aligning with the visual flow of data and processing within the AI agent system. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are visible on screen.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a wide shot of a dimly lit stage where NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, pants, and white sneakers, stands and gestures towards a large screen. The screen displays a technical diagram titled 'Vector Database' dated 'MARCH 18, 2024', with a purple 'y/ highlights' logo. Jensen Huang, speaking with a clear, authoritative voice, explains how data, which he describes as 'basically Nvidia's intelligence,' is taken to 'learn its meaning' and 're-index that knowledge into a new type of database called a vector database.' He elaborates that this process involves taking structured or unstructured data, learning and encoding its meaning to create an 'AI database' that one can 'talk to.' As he speaks, the camera zooms in on his expressive hand movements. The diagram on screen updates to include 'NeMo Retriever.' Subsequently, a 'Chat with PDF' panel appears on the right, showcasing a user interface with a chat window and document preview. Jensen uses this as an example of handling 'multi-modality data,' explaining how proprietary company PDFs can be encoded, while the interface demonstrates a conversation about speedup, citing NVIDIA T4 and H100 performance.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a technical diagram on a black background, illustrating the architecture of the NeMo Retriever system, featuring components like a Vector Database with RAPIDS cuVS Accelerated Indexing and Accelerated NN Search, connected to Embedding, Retrieval, and Text-to-SQL Microservices, all feeding into a central 'Plan' block. On the right, a 'Chat with PDF' interface is shown, displaying a document titled 'NVIDIA L4 Tensor Core GPU vaults ahead' with a bar chart and text, indicating an AI-driven document interaction capability. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' is visible in the top right corner, along with a purple 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right. The scene then transitions to NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, standing on a dark stage with a spotlight. He gestures expressively with his hands while speaking, holding a small black remote, explaining how the system can encode all of a user's PDF data, turning it into vectors stored inside a vector database, making it the proprietary information of their company. He emphasizes that once this proprietary information is vectorized, users can "chat to it," calling it a "smart database" where one can "chat with data." He humorously asks, "how much more enjoyable is that?" and gives an example of a software team chatting with a "bugs database," asking "how many bugs was there last night?" and "are we making any progress?" He then quips that after talking to the bugs database, "you need therapy," leading to the introduction of "another chatbot." The camera zooms out to reveal a wide shot of the stage, showing Jensen Huang as a small figure under a spotlight in a vast, dimly lit auditorium filled with an audience bathed in purple ambient lighting. The stage setup is minimalist, emphasizing the speaker and the presentation content. The camera then returns to a medium close-up of Jensen Huang, who continues to speak and gesture, formally introducing the system as "NeMo Retriever," explaining its core function: "to go retrieve information as quickly as possible." He demonstrates by saying, "And you just talk to it. Hey, retrieve me this information," describing how it brings the information back and asks, "do you mean this?"

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in his signature black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, presenting on a dark stage. He gestures towards a large screen displaying a complex 'Technical Diagram on Screen' illustrating an AI agent system, with a central 'Plan' block connected to various data sources and outputs, and a circular 'NVIDIA DHT' image. Jensen Huang speaks passionately, explaining that "so many companies" are "sitting on gold mines" of data and tools, which can be "turned into copilots" to "help us do things." He emphasizes that "just about every IT franchise, IT platform in the world" wants to build "their own copilots and their own chatbots." The scene then transitions to a black screen featuring the SAP and NVIDIA logos side by side, as Jensen Huang announces, "we're announcing that NVIDIA AI Foundry is working with some of the world's great companies." He highlights that "SAP generates 87% of the world's global commerce," and that "NVIDIA and SAP are building SAP Joule copilots using NVIDIA NeMo and DGX Cloud." Following this, a slide appears with the ServiceNow logo and tagline 'THE INTELLIGENT WORKFLOW COMPANY,' detailing its AI Research & Development, Engineering & Applied Science, and Global Cloud Services, all powered by NVIDIA since 2018, referencing DGX Cloud, NeMo, TensorRT, and NVIDIA AI Enterprise. Jensen Huang continues, mentioning "ServiceNow, they run..." before the segment ends. Throughout the presentation, the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' is visible in the top right corner, and the 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right, maintaining consistent branding.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, holding a small black remote and gesturing as he speaks with a clear, authoritative male voice. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a 'y/ highlights' logo are consistently visible on screen. He begins by stating that "85% of the world's Fortune 500 companies run their people and customer service operations on ServiceNow," as a large screen behind him displays a detailed diagram for ServiceNow, highlighting AI Research & Development, Engineering & Applied Science, and ServiceNow Global Cloud Services, all powered by NVIDIA since 2018, with references to DGX Cloud, NeMo, TensorRT, and NVIDIA AI Enterprise. He adds that they are "using NVIDIA AI Foundry to build ServiceNow assist virtual assistants." The screen then transitions to a simple black background with the ServiceNow and NVIDIA logos side by side. Huang continues, explaining that "Cohesity backs up the world's data," holding "hundreds of exabytes of data, over 10,000 companies," as the screen changes to a diagram for Cohesity, showcasing the Cohesity Data AI Agent and its integration with NVIDIA AI Enterprise, including RAPIDS, cuVS, NeMo Retriever, and NIM Microservices. He notes that "NVIDIA AI Foundry is working with them, helping them build their Gaia generative AI agent." The screen then displays the Cohesity and NVIDIA logos. Next, a diagram for Snowflake appears, illustrating 'DATA + AI – ALL ON NVIDIA' with applications like Snowflake Copilot and Snowflake Document AI, container services, and enterprise data accelerated by NVIDIA technologies such as NeMo Retriever and Triton Inference. Huang describes Snowflake as a company that "stores the world's digital warehouse in the cloud" and "serves over 3 billion queries a day for 10,000 enterprise customers," adding that "Snowflake is working with NVIDIA AI Foundry to build co-pilots with NVIDIA NeMo and NIMs." The screen transitions to the Snowflake and NVIDIA logos. Finally, a diagram for NetApp is shown, detailing how NetApp unlocks exabytes of data for secure, private Gen AI, with components like NetApp Customer Data, NVIDIA NeMo Retriever, and Gen AI with NetApp, culminating in the phrase 'NETAPP CUSTOMERS CAN NOW TALK TO THEIR DATA.' Huang states that "nearly half of the files in the world are stored on-prem on NetApp," and "NVIDIA AI Foundry is helping them build chatbots and co-pilots." The screen then displays the NetApp and NVIDIA logos.

audio_updated_entities.ServiceNow: The ServiceNow logo with the tagline 'THE INTELLIGENT WORKFLOW COMPANY,' displayed on a green and blue gradient background, detailing AI Research & Development, Engineering & Applied Science, and ServiceNow Global Cloud Services, all powered by NVIDIA since 2018, with references to DGX Cloud, NeMo, TensorRT, and NVIDIA AI Enterprise; Jensen Huang states that 85% of Fortune 500 companies run their people and customer service operations on ServiceNow, using NVIDIA AI Foundry to build 'assist virtual assistants'.
audio_updated_entities.Cohesity: The Cohesity logo, displayed on a black background, with a diagram titled 'Cohesity Gala – First to Provide AI-Powered Business Insights from Secondary Data,' showing integration with NVIDIA AI Enterprise, including RAPIDS, cuVS, NeMo Retriever, and NIM Microservices; Jensen Huang explains that Cohesity backs up hundreds of exabytes of data for over 10,000 companies and is working with NVIDIA AI Foundry to build their 'Gaia generative AI agent'.
audio_updated_entities.Snowflake: The Snowflake logo, displayed on a black background, with a diagram titled 'DATA + AI – ALL ON NVIDIA,' illustrating applications like Snowflake Copilot and Snowflake Document AI, container services, and enterprise data accelerated by NVIDIA technologies such as NeMo Retriever and Triton Inference; Jensen Huang describes Snowflake as storing the world's digital warehouse in the cloud, serving over 3 billion queries daily for 10,000 enterprise customers, and collaborating with NVIDIA AI Foundry to build co-pilots using NVIDIA NeMo and NIMs.
audio_updated_entities.NetApp: The NetApp logo, displayed on a black background, with a diagram titled 'NetApp Unlocks Exabytes of Data for Secure, Private Gen AI,' showing components like NetApp Customer Data, NVIDIA NeMo Retriever, and Gen AI with NetApp, culminating in the phrase 'NETAPP CUSTOMERS CAN NOW TALK TO THEIR DATA'; Jensen Huang highlights that nearly half of the world's files are stored on-prem on NetApp, and NVIDIA AI Foundry is assisting them in building chatbots and co-pilots.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, a man with gray hair in a black leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a remote on a dark stage. Behind him, NetApp and NVIDIA logos are displayed alongside the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a 'y/ highlights' logo. He gestures as he speaks clearly and confidently about AI pilots, vector databases, and retrievers using NVIDIA Nemo and Nims. The screen then transitions to a large diagram titled 'Dell AI Factory with NVIDIA,' showcasing an end-to-end enterprise AI solution. Jensen continues, emphasizing the "great partnership with Dell" and stating that anyone building chatbots and generative AI will need an "AI factory," asserting that "nobody is better at building end-to-end systems of very large scale for the enterprise than Dell." The camera zooms in on him before panning out to reveal a large audience in a purple-lit auditorium, attentively watching and then clapping enthusiastically. Jensen acknowledges the audience's engagement, noting that "Michael is here, he's happy to take your order," and then introduces "Ladies and gentlemen, Michael Dell," prompting more applause. The scene returns to Jensen Huang, who gestures toward a new diagram illustrating a circular workflow connecting AI, Omniverse Digital Twin, and Robotic System, with corresponding hardware images (DGX, OVX, AGX). He begins to discuss "the next wave of robotics," or "physical AI," explaining that previous AI discussions focused on "one computer" where "data comes into one computer, lots of the world..." The stage remains dark and minimalist, with focused lighting on the speaker and the large screen, creating a high-contrast, professional atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, holding a small black remote. He gestures expressively with both hands as he explains how AI systems will "watch us, understand what is happening and try to adapt it for themselves into the context," emphasizing that because AI can generalize with foundation models, "maybe these robots can also perform in the physical world fairly generally." White text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo are visible on screen. He continues, stating he just described "essentially what just happened in large language models, except the Chat GPT moment for robotics may be right around the corner." The scene then transitions to a wide shot, revealing a large green and white circular technical diagram behind him, illustrating the integration of AI, Omniverse Digital Twin, and Robotic System, with hardware components DGX, OVX, and AGX displayed. Gesturing towards the diagram, Jensen proudly states, "we've been building the end-to-end systems for robotics for some time. I'm super, super proud of the work." He points out, "We have the AI system DGX," and then introduces "the lower system which is called AGX for autonomous systems, the world's first robotics processor." He elaborates on AGX, noting that when they first built it, people questioned its purpose, but it's a "SOC, it's one chip, it's designed to be very low power, but it's designed for high-speed sensor processing and AI." The camera cuts back to a close-up as he continues to speak with animated hand gestures, explaining that "if you want to run transformer..." maintaining the professional, tech-forward atmosphere against the dark background.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, clad in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote. He speaks with a clear, confident voice, using dynamic hand gestures to emphasize his points as he explains that for "anything that moves," like a car running transformers, "we have the perfect computer for you. It's called the Jetson." The stage is dark, with a spotlight on him, creating a high-contrast, professional setting. On-screen text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo are displayed. He continues, detailing how "the DGX on top for training the AI, the Jetson is the autonomous processor, and in the middle, we need another computer." The camera occasionally zooms out to a wide shot, revealing Huang standing alone under the spotlight before a large, dimly lit audience bathed in purple ambient lighting. He engages the audience, discussing how "large language models have the benefit of you providing your examples and then doing reinforcement learning human feedback." He then poses, "What is the reinforcement learning human feedback of a robot?" answering that it's "reinforcement learning physical feedback," which is how a robot is aligned and learns articulation and manipulation capabilities to adapt to the laws of physics. He concludes by stating the need for "a simulation engine that represents the world digitally for the robot so that the robot has a gym to go learn how to be a robot. We call that..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, gesturing expressively on a dark stage, with the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo visible. Huang explains how the system will "redirect traffic" and provide "new waypoints" to robots and people, ensuring they "know exactly what to do." He then describes how a "warehouse" or "building" can be "talked to," even asking, "How are you feeling today?" as he details a system where Omniverse Cloud hosts virtual simulations and AI runs on DGX Cloud, all operating in real-time. The scene transitions to a wide shot revealing a large, dimly lit audience and a massive screen displaying a green and white circular diagram illustrating the workflow between 'AI', 'Omniverse Digital Twin', and 'Robotic System', with hardware components DGX, OVX, and AGX. This is followed by a split-screen view, showcasing a 3D simulation of a robotic arm moving a red object on a conveyor belt in a factory environment on the left, mirrored by a real-world robotic arm performing the identical task in a physical lab on the right. As Jensen says, "Let's take a look," the visuals shift to an overhead 3D simulation of a vast warehouse, complete with pallets, forklifts, and workers, which then transitions to a real-world warehouse scene where workers in safety vests and hard hats operate forklifts and manage pallets. A dramatic, orchestral background music begins, and a deeper male voice-over explains that "The future of heavy industry starts as a digital twin," where "AI agents helping robots, workers, and infrastructure navigate unpredictable events in complex industrial spaces will be built and evaluated first in sophisticated digital twins," specifically mentioning "This Omniverse Digital Twin of a 100,000 sq..." before the audio fades. The dark, focused environment with stage lighting consistently highlights the speaker and the large screen, emphasizing the seamless connection between simulation and physical operations.

segments.integrated_caption: The video, dated March 18, 2024, opens with a wide-angle view of a bustling warehouse, where workers in safety gear and forklifts move among stacks of boxes and pallets. A male narrator explains this is a "simulation environment" integrating "digital workers, AMRs running the Nvidia Isaac Perceptor stack, centralized activity maps... from 100 simulated ceiling mount cameras using Nvidia Metropolis, and AMR route planning with Nvidia Co-op." The scene transitions to a multi-camera tracking interface, displaying live feeds and a top-down digital map tracking 30 individuals, which the narrator clarifies is part of "software in loop testing of AI agents" to adapt to "real-world unpredictability." An overhead simulation view then overlays a green network of interconnected nodes and paths, dynamically adjusting to movements, with red lines indicating conflicts. The narrator states, "Here, an incident occurs along this AMR's planned route, blocking its path," accompanied by a distinct crash sound. The camera zooms into an aisle, showing a forklift on a green-lit path and a worker, as an 'Agent Response' pop-up appears, querying an unusual situation in aisle 3. The system alerts that "A shelf collapsed and the boxes fell from shelving at 3:30 PM, leaving boxes blocking the aisle," confirmed by a live video from Camera_048 with green bounding boxes. The narrator explains that "Nvidia Metropolis updates and sends a real-time occupancy map to Co-op, where a new optimal route is calculated," enabling the AMR to "see around corners and improve its mission efficiency." The brightly lit warehouse floor is marked with navigation lines, and the 'y/ highlights' logo remains visible, with the narrator adding that "With generative AI powered Metropolis Vision Foundation models, operators can even ask questions using natural language."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a wide-angle view of a bustling warehouse on March 18, 2024, where workers in safety gear move among stacks of boxes and pallets, while forklifts navigate the aisles. The scene transitions to a multi-camera tracking interface, displaying a grid of live feeds from various angles across the warehouse, alongside a top-down digital map that tracks 30 individuals with color-coded dots and numbers, illustrating real-time movement and spatial awareness. The perspective then shifts to an overhead simulation view, overlaying a green network of interconnected nodes and paths across the warehouse floor, representing an AI-driven logistics optimization system. This network dynamically adjusts as workers and forklifts move, with red lines indicating potential conflicts or blocked routes. The camera zooms into specific aisles, highlighting a forklift navigating a green-lit path, while a worker in a yellow hard hat and blue vest walks alongside. A pop-up interface appears, labeled 'Agent Response,' querying if an unusual situation occurred in aisle 3. The system responds with a text alert: 'A shelf collapsed and the boxes fell from shelving at 3:30 PM, leaving boxes blocking the aisle,' accompanied by a live video feed from Camera_048 showing the incident with green bounding boxes around the fallen boxes. The environment is brightly lit with industrial lighting, and the floor is marked with yellow and green lines for navigation. The 'y/ highlights' logo remains in the bottom right corner throughout, and the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' is displayed in the top right.

audio_updated_entities.Forklifts: Blue and black forklifts navigating the warehouse aisles, lifting and transporting pallets of boxes, with one shown following a green-lit path. The narrator identifies these as 'AMRs' (Autonomous Mobile Robots) running the 'Nvidia Isaac Perceptor stack' and benefiting from 'AMR route planning with Nvidia Co-op' to 'see around corners and improve its mission efficiency'.
audio_updated_entities.Multi-Camera Tracking Interface: A split-screen display showing a grid of live camera feeds from various warehouse angles on the left, and a top-down digital map on the right with color-coded dots and numbers tracking 30 individuals in real time. This interface is powered by 'centralized activity maps of the entire warehouse from 100 simulated ceiling mount cameras using Nvidia Metropolis'.
audio_updated_entities.AI-Driven Logistics Network Overlay: A green network of interconnected nodes and paths overlaid on the warehouse floor, dynamically adjusting to worker and forklift movements, with red lines indicating blocked routes or conflicts. This system is responsible for 'AMR route planning with Nvidia Co-op' and uses 'Nvidia Metropolis' to update and send a 'real-time occupancy map' for calculating 'optimal routes'.
audio_updated_entities.Warehouse Aisle 3 Incident: A specific aisle where a shelf collapsed, causing boxes to fall and block the path, as detected and reported by the AI system via Camera_048 with green bounding boxes highlighting the fallen boxes. The narrator describes this as 'an incident' occurring along an 'AMR's planned route, blocking its path,' accompanied by a distinct crash sound.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a technical diagram illustrating a digital twin system for a warehouse, showing real-world cameras and robots feeding data into NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud, then to NVIDIA DGX Cloud, and finally to applications like Metropolis MTMC and cuOpt Route Planning, all visualized with green cloud icons and directional arrows. A male voice, identified as NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, explains that this system understands nuanced activity, offers immediate insights, and that all sensor data is created in simulation and passed to real-time AI running as NVIDIA Inference Microservices (NIMs). He continues, stating that when the AI is ready for deployment in the physical twin, the real warehouse, Metropolis and Isaac NIMs connect to real sensors for continuous improvement of both the digital twin and AI models. The diagram transitions to a 3D-rendered warehouse environment, where animated workers in safety gear and forklifts move among stacks of boxes and conveyor belts, demonstrating the simulation. Jensen Huang, dressed in his signature black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, then appears on a dark stage, gesturing expressively as he speaks. He emphasizes that a future facility, whether a warehouse, factory, or building, will be software-defined, and the software is tested within the digital twin. He points out that the animated robots seen in the simulation are running their own autonomous robotic stack, and the integration of software for robotic systems in the future is achieved through digital twins. The on-screen text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo remain visible throughout.

The diagram transitions to a 3D-rendered warehouse environment, where animated workers in safety gear and forklifts move among stacks of boxes and conveyor belts. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote, gesturing expressively on a dark stage with focused lighting against a minimalist background. On-screen, 'MARCH 18, 2024' is displayed in the top right, and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right. Jensen's clear voice explains how NVIDIA has made "Omniverse a lot easier to access," announcing the creation of "Omniverse Cloud APIs" – "four simple API and a channel" – allowing applications to connect, promising "magical digital twin capability." As he speaks, the scene transitions to a technical diagram on a black screen, illustrating an 'Application' connected via 'USD APIs' and 'Omniverse Channel APIs' to the 'NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud' (represented by a green cloud icon with a hexagonal logo). Jensen continues, revealing that Omniverse has also been "turned into an AI," integrated with the ability to "chat USD," which he identifies as "universal scene description," the complex language Omniverse has been taught. The diagram evolves to show additional components: 'Edify', 'ChatUSD', and 'DeepSearch' blocks, each receiving a 'Prompt'. Jensen elaborates that users can "speak to it in English" to "directly generate USD" and receive responses in English, even though Omniverse processes USD. The diagram shows 'Edify' outputting '3D USD 360 HDRI Materials', 'ChatUSD' outputting 'USD', and 'DeepSearch' also outputting 'USD' while connecting to a 'Data Lake', emphasizing the architecture of NVIDIA's cloud-based 3D content creation and AI integration ecosystem. Jensen concludes by mentioning the ability to "look for information in this world semantically."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang on stage, dressed in a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote. He gestures expressively as he speaks, with the stage lighting focused on him against a dark, minimalist background. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' is displayed in the top right corner, and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo is in the bottom right. The scene transitions to a technical diagram on a black screen, showing an 'Application' block connected via 'USD APIs' and 'Omniverse Channel APIs' to the 'NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud'—a green cloud icon with a hexagonal logo. The diagram evolves to include additional components: 'Edify', 'ChatUSD', and 'DeepSearch', each receiving a 'Prompt' and outputting '3D USD 360 HDRI Materials', 'USD', and 'USD' respectively, with 'DeepSearch' also connecting to a 'Data Lake'. The diagram illustrates a workflow where these services feed into the Omniverse Cloud, which then interfaces with the Application. The visual remains static for the remainder of the segment, emphasizing the architecture of NVIDIA's cloud-based 3D content creation and AI integration ecosystem.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, gesturing expressively with a small remote as he speaks. He explains how semantic encoding now applies to scenes, allowing AI to find specific objects or conditions and collaborate in 3D design, simulation, and generation. White text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo are visible. As he continues, the camera zooms out to reveal the 'SIEMENS' and 'NVIDIA' logos prominently displayed on the large screen behind him, with a dimly lit audience in the foreground. Huang announces a 'great partnership' with Siemens, describing them as the 'world's largest industrial engineering and operations platform' and highlighting heavy industries as a 'final frontier of IT.' He states that Siemens is building the 'industrial metaverse' and is connecting their 'crown jewel accelerator' to NVIDIA Omniverse. The scene then transitions to a 3D-rendered industrial facility, a brightly lit warehouse with tall metal shelving and stacked boxes. Two warehouse workers, a woman with a tablet and a man with a clipboard, both in yellow safety vests, white hard hats, and gloves, walk through an aisle, discussing and gesturing. Upbeat, futuristic music plays, and a voiceover declares, 'Siemens technology is transformed everyday for everyone,' as the camera follows the workers, emphasizing the facility's scale.
