segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a Siemens Teamcenter X interface, showcasing a detailed 3D model of a coffee machine's internal components, as a male voice introduces Teamcenter X as their leading product lifecycle management software from the Siemens Xcelerator platform, used daily by customers to develop and deliver products at scale. The scene transitions to a woman in a blue lab coat and a man in a white shirt and tie collaborating over a laptop displaying a green spherical conceptual design, while the male voice explains they are bringing the real and digital worlds closer by integrating NVIDIA AI and Omniverse technologies into Teamcenter X. A black screen with the NVIDIA Omniverse logo appears, followed by a diagram illustrating the integration of Siemens Xcelerator, RTX Render, and NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud, emphasizing USD data flow, with the voice detailing how Omniverse APIs enable data interoperability and physics-based rendering for industrial-scale design. The narrative then shifts to a real-world shipyard with large cranes and a massive 'HD Hyundai' ship under construction, transitioning into a highly detailed 3D simulation of the same ship within Siemens Teamcenter X, revealing its intricate internal systems. The male voice identifies HD Hyundai as a market leader in sustainable ship manufacturing, building ammonia and hydrogen-powered ships often comprising over 7 million discrete parts. A diagram shows a user inputting a prompt ('atlantic ocean waves, remote, photography, ocean, sea, morning sky') into Siemens Xcelerator, connecting to RTX Render and NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud via Edify API, resulting in a photorealistic rendering of the ship in a maritime environment. The voice concludes by stating that Omniverse APIs allow Teamcenter X to unify and visualize these massive engineering datasets interactively, integrating generative AI to create 3D objects or HDRI backgrounds for project context, as the final frames show the ship's propeller and rudder in the simulation, with a worker in a hard hat observing the digital twin.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video begins with a close-up of a Siemens Teamcenter X interface, displaying a detailed 3D model of a coffee machine's internal components, including water pumps and heating systems, with a user navigating through the design tree. The scene transitions to a woman in a blue lab coat using a laptop to view a 3D model of a coffee machine, followed by a man in a white shirt and tie collaborating with her, both focused on a screen showing a green spherical structure with windows, likely a conceptual design. The focus then shifts to a black screen with the NVIDIA Omniverse logo, followed by a diagram illustrating the integration of Siemens Xcelerator, RTX Render, and NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud, emphasizing USD data flow. The narrative progresses to a real-world shipyard with large cranes and a massive ship under construction, branded 'HD Hyundai,' transitioning into a highly detailed 3D simulation of the same ship within Siemens Teamcenter X, showcasing its intricate internal systems and exterior. The video then displays a diagram of a user inputting a prompt ('atlantic ocean waves, remote, photography, ocean, sea, morning sky') into Siemens Xcelerator, which connects to RTX Render and NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud via Edify API, resulting in a photorealistic rendering of the ship in a maritime environment. The final frames show the ship's propeller and rudder in the simulation, with a worker in a hard hat observing the digital twin, highlighting the seamless integration of real-world engineering with digital simulation for maritime design and visualization.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a detailed 3D digital twin of a large ship's internal structure, rendered in grayscale within Siemens Teamcenter X, showcasing intricate piping and machinery. A male voice, later identified as Roland Busch, CEO of Siemens, describes this as an "ultra intuitive photoreal physics-based digital twin that eliminates waste and errors, delivering huge savings in cost and time." The view transitions to the ship's exterior, revealing the 'HD HYUNDAI' branding, as the voice continues, emphasizing that "we are building this for collaboration" across Siemens Xcelerator tools and teams. A technical diagram then illustrates the integration of Siemens Xcelerator with NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud via USD APIs and Omniverse Channel APIs, while the voice highlights bringing "accelerated computing, generative AI, and Omniverse integration across the Siemens Xcelerator portfolio." Following a burst of applause, the narrative moves to a real-world shipyard where a worker in a hard hat inspects the ship's hull, then to a simulation of the shipyard with animated workers and cranes. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, walks onto a presentation stage, gesturing toward a massive screen displaying the HD Hyundai shipyard digital twin. He then humorously identifies the previous speaker, stating, "The professional voice actor happens to be a good friend of mine, Roland Busch, who happens to be the CEO of Siemens," eliciting further applause from the dimly lit audience under purple lighting. The video culminates in a wide shot of the stage with Jensen Huang before the expansive digital twin, emphasizing the scale and sophistication of the collaboration.

The narrative then moves to a real-world shipyard environment, where a worker in a hard hat inspects the ship's hull, followed by a simulation of the shipyard with animated workers and cranes. The scene transitions to a presentation stage where NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket and black pants, walks across the stage, gesturing toward a massive screen displaying the HD Hyundai shipyard digital twin. The video highlights the seamless integration of digital twin technology, real-world shipbuilding, and AI-driven simulation, culminating in a wide shot of the stage with Jensen Huang standing before the expansive digital twin of the shipyard, emphasizing the scale and sophistication of the collaboration between Siemens and NVIDIA.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, stands on a dark, focused stage, gesturing with a small remote as he speaks with a clear, confident voice. He explains how connecting Omniverse into a workflow, from design to engineering, manufacturing planning, and digital twin operations, leads to immense productivity by allowing everyone to operate on the 'same ground truth' without data exchange issues. He emphasizes that this applies across design, art, architecture, engineering, and marketing departments. As he concludes this point, a subtle, almost futuristic sound effect plays, and he announces, "Let's take a look at how Nissan has integrated Omniverse into their workflow." The scene then transitions to a technical diagram on screen, featuring the Nissan logo and illustrating the integration of a 3D car model, headlight assembly, car interior, and a red sports car with NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud via USD and Omniverse Channel APIs. The diagram remains on screen, emphasizing the seamless integration of design and simulation tools. A wide shot reveals a large, dimly lit audience bathed in purple light, attentively watching the presentation. The video then cuts to a cinematic 3D animation of a red Nissan sports car driving across a reflective body of water under a dramatic sky, accompanied by a powerful, cinematic orchestral score, with the 'Media.Monks' credit visible. Finally, a white handbag with a triangular logo is shown, flanked by two disassembled car chassis, credited to 'Configit', symbolizing the fusion of automotive engineering and fashion design.

Finally, the video shows a white handbag with a triangular logo, flanked by two disassembled car chassis with wheels and mechanical components, credited to 'Configit' in the bottom left, symbolizing the fusion of automotive engineering and fashion design.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dynamic, upbeat electronic-orchestral background track as a beige puffer jacket from Brickland is shown with a digital overlay of a black branch and numerical data. The scene quickly transitions to a red sports car driving through a mountainous landscape, maintaining the energetic music. A colorful, multi-hued SUV from BITONE is then customized in a 3D interface, accompanied by Chinese dialogue where a male voice states, '每一种颜色都能使这款车型焕发出独特的魅力' (Every color can make this car model radiate unique charm). A female voice responds, '我喜欢有质感些的灰色' (I like a more textured gray), to which the male voice affirms, '非常好的选择' (A very good choice), highlighting the interactive customization process. The music continues as a white handbag from Configit, featuring a gold triangular clasp, is displayed with a user interface showing incompatible options for leather color and backdrop. A light blue Nissan sports car is then seen parked in front of a modern house, with a detailed lighting configuration panel visible. The Brickland jacket reappears in multiple color variations, followed by a sleek black Lotus Eletre SUV that changes color to green and then dark gray, with a small animated character in a racing suit. A L'Oréal Revitalift Filler serum bottle from WPP & L'Oréal is shown with a text box prompting for an 'Abstract background of geometric crystals in diffused magenta and violet hues,' then a WPP hand soap bottle with animated lilies and daisies. An Adidas sneaker by Media.Monks with a Chicago skyline design is briefly shown, before a silver Hyundai car from 3DEXCITE is customized in a 3D environment with a prompt for a 'Breathtaking swiss mountain landscape.' The segment concludes with a Land Rover Defender from Defender & Accenture in a snowy mountain environment, featuring a text overlay 'Let’s see it handle extreme conditions,' all set to the continuous, driving background music. Throughout, 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are consistently displayed.

A light blue Nissan sports car is then shown parked in front of a modern house, with a detailed lighting configuration panel visible on the right. The video returns to the Brickland jacket, now displayed in multiple color variations, before showcasing a sleek black Lotus Eletre SUV, which changes color to green and then to a dark gray, with a small animated character in a racing suit appearing in the corner. The segment continues with a L'Oréal Revitalift Filler serum bottle, where a text box prompts for an 'Abstract background of geometric crystals in diffused magenta and violet hues,' followed by a hand soap bottle with animated lilies and daisies. The final scene features a Land Rover Defender in a snowy mountain environment, with a text overlay reading 'Let’s see it handle extreme conditions,' emphasizing the vehicle's rugged capabilities. Throughout, the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are consistently displayed in the top right and bottom right corners, respectively, indicating a curated highlight reel of digital design and simulation advancements.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an upbeat, futuristic, and slightly dramatic background music as a dynamic 3D simulation showcases a Land Rover Defender navigating a snowy mountain landscape, with the text 'Let’s see it handle extreme conditions' emphasizing its rugged capabilities. The scene transitions to a woman, identified as 'Katana', wearing a white head-mounted device, interacting with a virtual red Nissan sports car in a modern, brick-walled office environment. She uses hand gestures to change the car’s color and environment, with a floating UI panel displaying customization options like 'Paint' and 'Trim'. The car is then seen in a virtual 'Blue Ridge Mountains at sunrise' setting, before a full-scale 3D model of the red car appears in the physical office space, demonstrating augmented reality integration. As the music fades slightly, NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, and white sneakers, takes a dark, minimalist stage with focused lighting. He begins speaking, stating, "Today we're announcing that Omniverse Cloud streams to the Vision Pro," which is met with audience applause. He gestures toward a large screen displaying a diagram connecting 'Apple Vision Pro' to 'NVIDIA Omniverse Cloud', illustrating a collaborative AR/VR ecosystem. Jensen Huang continues, emphasizing the immersive design and engineering workflows, adding, "It is very, very strange that you walk around virtual doors when I was getting out of that car. And everybody does it. It is really, really quite amazing. Vision Pro connected to Omniverse, portal." The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and 'y/ highlights' logo are consistently displayed in the corners.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark stage, gesturing expressively as he speaks. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' is visible in the top right, with a 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right. He discusses the integration of various tools into Omniverse, enabling a sophisticated workflow. The scene transitions to a technical diagram illustrating a closed-loop autonomous vehicle development workflow, featuring two BYD vehicles—a red and a teal SUV—alongside the BYD logo on the left. Jensen emphasizes that 'everything that moves will be robotic' and that the automotive industry is one of the largest. The central diagram shows a circular flow connecting 'MSFNET ROADNET PLANNET' (linked to DGX), 'DRIVE SIM IN OMNIVERSE' (linked to OVX), and 'DRIVE AV STACK' (linked to AGX). He explains that NVIDIA builds 'the robotic stack from top to bottom,' encompassing the computer system and self-driving car applications. On the right, three panels display a 360-degree camera view, a simulated 360-degree view from Omniverse, and a close-up of the 'DRIVE THOR ASIL-D AV COMPUTER & STACK' hardware. Jensen reveals that they will be shipping this technology in Mercedes and JLR vehicles, highlighting that these 'autonomous robotic systems are software defined,' involving 'computer vision,' 'artificial intelligence,' 'control and planning,' and other 'very complicated technology' that takes years to refine. He concludes by stating that NVIDIA opens up their entire stack for the automotive industry.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, gestures expressively on a dark stage, holding a small black remote. He speaks with a clear, confident voice, explaining NVIDIA's philosophy of building and then opening up their technology for accessibility, stating, "This is just the way we work... we open it up so everybody can access it." As the camera pulls back to reveal a large, dimly lit audience bathed in purple light, a large screen behind him displays a technical diagram with green boxes labeled 'DGX', 'OVX', 'AGX', 'DRIVE SIM IN OMNIVERSE', 'DRIVE AV STACK', and 'MSFNET ROADNET PLANNET' connected in a circular flow. Jensen elaborates on NVIDIA's offerings, referring to their "computer" – which he describes as "the world's only full functional safe ASIL-D system that can run AI" – and their operating system or data centers. The diagram on screen then expands to include 3D-rendered vehicles, specifically a red BYD SUV and a teal SUV, as Jensen announces that BYD, "the world's largest EV company," is adopting NVIDIA's "next generation AV computer," named Thor, designed for transformer engines. Following his announcement, the audience erupts in enthusiastic applause. The screen then transitions to a broader 'NVIDIA Robotics Platform Adoption' view, showcasing statistics like '1.3M Robotics Developers' and '6,000 Companies Developing on Orin', alongside a circular diagram featuring 'ISAAC PERCEPTOR', 'ISAAC SIM IN OMNIVERSE', and 'ISAAC ROBOTIC STACK', illustrated with images of a warehouse robot, a forklift, and a Jetson Orin robotic computer. The minimalist stage remains dark with focused lighting on Jensen, while the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a 'y/ highlights' logo are visible on screen.

The circular diagram now includes 'ISAAC PERCEPTOR', 'ISAAC SIM IN OMNIVERSE', and 'ISAAC ROBOTIC STACK', with images of a warehouse robot, a forklift, and a Jetson Orin robotic computer. The stage setup remains minimalist and dark, with focused lighting on the speaker and the large screen, while the audience remains in shadow. On-screen text in the top right corner reads 'MARCH 18, 2024', and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo is in the bottom right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a detailed presentation slide titled 'NVIDIA Robotics Platform Adoption' dated 'MARCH 18, 2024', displaying a circular diagram connecting 'ISAAC PERCEPTOR', 'ISAAC SIM IN OMNIVERSE', and 'ISAAC ROBOTIC STACK' to hardware components like 'DGX', 'OVX', and 'AGX'. On the left, statistics highlight '1.3M Robotics Developers', '100,000 ROS Developers', and '6,000 Companies Developing on Orin', while the bottom left shows 3D-rendered robots on a grid. Panels on the right display a 360-degree camera view, a warehouse simulation, and a close-up of the 'JETSON ORIN ROBOTIC COMPUTER & STACK', with the 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, then takes the dark stage, speaking into a microphone headset and gesturing with a small black remote. He emphasizes the significant number of "over a million robotics developers" and expresses pride in the "Jetson, this robotics computer," noting the "insane" amount of software it supports due to its "100% CUDA compatible" nature. As the camera alternates between close-ups of him and wide shots showing him on stage with the unchanged presentation screen behind him, he explains that "everything that we do in our company is in service of our developers," enabling them to bring "incredible capability to this little tiny computer we call Jetson." He then announces an "incredibly advanced new SDK" called "Isaac Perceptor," explaining that while "most of the robots today are pre-programmed," following "digital rails" or "April tags," future robots will "have perception," allowing for easier programming.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, stands on a dark, minimally lit stage, holding a small black remote. He gestures dynamically as he explains how robots can navigate adaptively from point A to point B by programming only waypoints, allowing the entire route and environment to be reprogrammed, unlike pre-programmed AGVs that can 'gum up' if boxes fall. The on-screen text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo remain visible. The scene transitions to a large presentation screen displaying the 'NVIDIA Robotics Platform Adoption' diagram, featuring a circular workflow with 'ISAAC PERCEPTOR', 'ISAAC SIM IN OMNIVERSE', and 'ISAAC ROBOTIC STACK' connected to hardware components 'DGX', 'OVX', and 'AGX'. Jensen highlights that 'ISAAC PERCEPTOR' provides 'incredible state-of-the-art vision odometry, 3D reconstruction, and depth perception' for environmental awareness. The diagram also shows '1.3M Robotics Developers', '100,000 ROS Developers', and '6,000 Companies Developing on Orin', alongside real-world robotics applications. The screen then shifts to a new diagram focused on manipulation, with 'POSE GRASP MANIPULATE' replacing 'ISAAC PERCEPTOR', and a blue Yaskawa robotic arm appearing on the left. Jensen continues, noting that the most used robot today is the manipulator, and discusses the computer vision, AI, control, and geometry-aware path planning algorithms. Logos for Yaskawa, Universal Robots, PickNik, Solomon, Ready Robotics, and Franka are displayed at the bottom, as Jensen maintains a confident and energetic presence.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang on a dark stage, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote. He gestures dynamically as he speaks, emphasizing points with hand movements. The stage is minimally lit, focusing attention on him. The on-screen text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo remain in the top right and bottom right corners, respectively. The scene transitions to a large presentation screen displaying the 'NVIDIA Robotics Platform Adoption' diagram, which features a circular workflow with green boxes labeled 'ISAAC PERCEPTOR', 'ISAAC SIM IN OMNIVERSE', and 'ISAAC ROBOTIC STACK', connected to hardware components 'DGX', 'OVX', and 'AGX'. On the right, three panels show real-world robotics applications: a warehouse with a person and a robot, a robotic arm in a factory, and a close-up of the 'Jetson Orin Robotic Computer & Stack'. The diagram also highlights '1.3M Robotics Developers', '100,000 ROS Developers', and '6,000 Companies Developing on Orin'. Jensen Huang continues to gesture toward the screen, explaining the ecosystem. The scene then shifts to a new diagram focused on manipulation, with 'POSE GRASP MANIPULATE' replacing 'ISAAC PERCEPTOR', and a blue Yaskawa robotic arm appearing on the left. The right panels now show a robotic arm in a simulation, a white robotic arm in a box, and the same Jetson Orin hardware. Logos for Yaskawa, Universal Robots, PickNik, Solomon, Ready Robotics, and Franka appear at the bottom. Jensen Huang remains the central figure, actively engaging with the audience and the content on screen, maintaining a confident and energetic presence throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a technical diagram on a black background, illustrating a robotics workflow featuring a circular flow connecting 'POSE GRASP MANIPULATE', 'ISAAC SIM IN OMNIVERSE', and 'ISAAC MANIPULATOR STACK', linked to hardware components DGX, OVX, and AGX. A blue Yaskawa robotic arm is on the left, while the right shows a 360-degree camera view, a robotic arm simulation, and a Jetson Orin Robotic Computer & Stack. Logos for Yaskawa, Universal Robots, PickNik, Solomon, Ready Robotics, and Franka, along with the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a 'y/ highlights' logo, are visible. NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, wearing a black textured leather jacket, takes the stage, holding a remote and gesturing as he articulately explains that these "incredibly computationally intensive" processes have been made "CUDA accelerated," introducing the "world's first CUDA accelerated motion planner that is geometry aware." He elaborates on its "excellent perception for pose estimation of a 3D object," emphasizing its ability to understand and "imagine what's around and how best to grap it." As he speaks, the background alternates between 'PICKNIK' and 'SOLOMON' logos. Jensen Huang names the system "Isaac Manipulator," noting that its foundation pose, grip foundation, and articulation algorithms are now available and run on NVIDIA computers. The camera pulls back to reveal him under a spotlight, addressing a large, dimly lit audience bathed in purple light. He then transitions, stating, "We are starting to do some really great work in the next generation of robotics," which "will likely be a humanoid robotics." This statement coincides with a cut to a new diagram for humanoid robotics, replacing the previous one. This new diagram features a circular flow with 'GROOT FOUNDATION MODEL', 'ISAAC LAB IN OMNIVERSE', and 'GROOT STACK', connected to DGX, OVX, and AGX. On the left, a black and teal exoskeleton humanoid robot is shown, while the right panels display a GROOT model diagram, a simulation of humanoid robots navigating terrain, and two Jetson Thor Robotic Computer & Stack units, with the date and highlights logo remaining.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a detailed technical diagram on a black background, illustrating the NVIDIA robotics ecosystem centered around the GROOT foundation model, showing a circular flow between the GROOT FOUNDATION MODEL, ISAAC LAB IN OMNIVERSE, and GROOT STACK. On the right, a flowchart depicts DGX feeding Language, Videos, and Demonstrations into the GROOT Model for a Humanoid Robot, alongside a simulation of a humanoid robot navigating block-filled terrain and JETSON THOR circuit boards. A black and teal Humanoid Robot (GROOT) stands on the left, while 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo are displayed. As this diagram is shown, NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang's clear, articulate voice begins, explaining the "necessary technology to imagine generalized human robotics." He states that "human robotics is likely easier" due to "a lot more imitation training data that we can provide the robots because we are constructed in a very similar way." The scene then transitions to Jensen Huang himself, a man with gray hair, wearing a black textured leather jacket over a black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, standing on a dark, minimally lit stage. Holding a small black remote and gesturing with his left hand, he continues to speak, emphasizing that "humanoid robotics will be much more useful in our world because we created the world" for human interaction. He elaborates that "workstations, manufacturing, and logistics" were "designed for for humans," making "these human robotics... much more productive to deploy." He then describes NVIDIA's effort in creating "the entire stack," starting with "a foundation model that learns from watching video, human examples," which could be in "video form" or "virtual reality form." The date and logo remain on screen throughout his address.

On the right side, three panels are vertically aligned: the top panel shows a flowchart labeled 'DGX' with inputs like Language, Videos, and Demonstrations feeding into the GROOT Model, which outputs Actions and Observations for a Humanoid Robot; the middle panel displays a simulation of a humanoid robot navigating a complex, block-filled terrain in a grayscale environment; and the bottom panel shows two circuit boards labeled 'JETSON THOR ROBOTIC COMPUTER & STACK'. On the left, a black and teal humanoid robot, identified as the Humanoid Robot (GROOT), stands facing forward. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' is displayed in the top right corner, and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo is in the bottom right. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, wearing his signature black leather jacket, black shirt, and glasses, gesturing expressively on a dark stage, with 'MARCH 18, 2024' and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo visible. He speaks about the 'Isaac reinforcement learning gym' which allows a 'humanoid robot' to learn to adapt to the physical world, and introduces an 'incredible computer' that will power both robotic cars and a 'humanoid robot called Thor,' specifically 'designed for transformer engines.' As he speaks, a large technical diagram appears, illustrating a circular workflow between 'GROOT FOUNDATION MODEL,' 'ISAAC LAB IN OMNIVERSE,' and 'GROOT STACK,' connected to hardware components DGX, OVX, and AGX, with a humanoid robot (GROOT) on the left and images of Jetson Thor hardware on the right. Jensen then cues a video, leading into a rapid montage of historical and modern robotics and AI footage, including a black-and-white robot arm, a Stanford Research Institute diagram, a NASA lunar lander, and a grid of modern robotics demonstrations. This montage is underscored by dramatic, inspiring music and a female voiceover stating, "It's not enough for humans to imagine. We have to invent and explore and push beyond what's been done. We create smarter and faster." The segment concludes with a 3D simulation in Isaac Sim, showing a white robot arm manipulating a yellow block in a blue grid environment, with an inset labeled 'NVBLOX Example' displaying a hand gesture and its 3D reconstruction, all set to the continuing music and voiceover.

The scene transitions to a large technical diagram on screen, showing a circular workflow between 'GROOT FOUNDATION MODEL', 'ISAAC LAB IN OMNIVERSE', and 'GROOT STACK', connected to hardware components DGX, OVX, and AGX, with a humanoid robot (GROOT) on the left and images of Jetson Thor hardware on the right. This is followed by a rapid montage of historical robotics and AI footage, including a black-and-white robot arm, a Stanford Research Institute diagram, a NASA lunar lander, and a grid of modern robotics demonstrations. The segment concludes with a 3D simulation in Isaac Sim, showing a white robot arm manipulating a yellow block in a blue grid environment, with an inset labeled 'NVBLOX Example' displaying a hand gesture and its 3D reconstruction.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a real-world 'Isaac Manipulator' robotic arm, featuring a black and white end-effector, precisely lifting a wooden block and interacting with a mug on a black table, accompanied by mechanical whirs and clicks. A narrator begins, stating, "We push it to fail, so it can learn." This transitions to a simulated 'Isaac Sim in Omniverse Digital Twin' environment where a robotic arm sorts colored blocks on a checkered floor, as the narration continues, "We teach it, then help it teach itself." A split-screen then shows 'DexPilot,' with a human hand demonstrating a task on the left and a robot mimicking it on the right, while the narrator explains, "We broaden its understanding to take on new challenges." Multiple white robotic arms perform coordinated tasks in a grid-like factory simulation labeled 'Leonardo,' with the narration emphasizing, "With absolute precision and succeed." The scene shifts to 'Clara Holoscan,' a split-screen of a surgical robot and a digital twin of a human organ, as the narrator states, "We make it perceive." A warehouse simulation, 'Isaac Perceptor,' shows a robot navigating aisles with thermal overlays, paralleled by a real-world robot, as the narration adds, "And move." Autonomous vehicle simulation, 'DRIVE Sim in Omniverse Digital Twin,' displays sensor data and a realistic city street, followed by a first-person driving perspective with AI-generated navigation paths, with the narrator concluding, "And even reason, so it can share our world with us." The video then shows a red and black 'ElectriCSheep' tractor in a field and a 'Pegatron' factory simulation of a robotic arm performing assembly. 'Orbita for Surgery' presents a robotic arm alongside a surgeon, demonstrating surgical precision. The segment concludes with a top-down view of a 'Covariant OFER' robotic arm sorting produce, including tomatoes and limes, and a large-scale 'Amazon Robotics' warehouse simulation featuring blue robotic carts moving boxes through an automated facility, all set to a consistent, slightly dramatic and inspiring background music with continuous mechanical sound effects.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment opens with a real-world robotic arm, labeled 'Isaac Manipulator,' performing precise tasks on a black table, including lifting a wooden block and interacting with a mug. The scene transitions to a simulated environment, 'Isaac Sim in Omniverse Digital Twin,' where a robotic arm sorts colored blocks on a checkered floor, demonstrating AI-driven object categorization. The narrative then shifts to a split-screen view: on the left, a human hand demonstrates a task, while on the right, a robot mimics the action, labeled 'DexPilot,' showcasing imitation learning. This is followed by a simulation of multiple robotic arms in a grid-like factory setup, labeled 'Leonardo,' performing coordinated tasks. The video then presents a split-screen of a surgical robot and a digital twin of a human organ, labeled 'Clara Holoscan,' highlighting medical robotics. Next, a warehouse simulation, 'Isaac Perceptor,' shows a robot navigating aisles with thermal overlays, while a real-world robot follows a similar path. The segment transitions to autonomous vehicle simulation, 'DRIVE Sim in Omniverse Digital Twin,' with a split view of sensor data and a realistic city street, followed by a first-person driving perspective with AI-generated navigation paths. The video then shows a tractor in a field, labeled 'ElectriCSheep,' and a simulation of a robotic arm in a factory, labeled 'Pegatron,' performing assembly tasks. A split-screen of a robotic arm and a surgeon, labeled 'Orbita for Surgery,' demonstrates surgical precision. The final scenes include a top-down view of a robotic arm sorting produce, labeled 'Covariant OFER,' and a large-scale warehouse simulation, 'Amazon Robotics,' with blue robotic carts moving boxes through an automated facility, emphasizing industrial automation and digital twin technology.

segments.integrated_caption: Amidst an upbeat, futuristic musical score, the video opens with a high-angle view of an 'Omniverse Digital Twin' simulation of an 'Amazon Robotics' warehouse, where blue robotic carts efficiently move boxes. A female voice declares, "This is where inspiration leads us. The next frontier." The scene transitions to a lab where a woman in a gray shirt interacts with a white humanoid robot, which mimics her hand wave, with a 3D simulation displayed on a large screen in the background. The narrator then introduces, "This is Nvidia Project Groot," as a wide shot reveals a spacious lab with multiple humanoid robots—some teal and black, others white and gray—navigating an obstacle course under the observation of engineers. The female voice continues, describing 'NVIDIA Project GROOT' as "A general purpose foundation model for humanoid robot learning." The focus shifts to a white 'Apptronik' humanoid robot with black gloves holding a laptop in a kitchen setting, followed by a woman in a teal shirt guiding a teal 'Agility Robotics' robot with a tablet. The narrator explains, "The Groot model takes multimodal instructions and past interactions as input, and produces the next action for the robot to execute." A diagram illustrates 'NVIDIA Project GROOT' processing task prompts from language, videos, and demonstrations to generate actions for a humanoid robot, with feedback from observations. The female voice adds, "We developed Isaac Lab, a robot learning application to train Groot on Omniverse Isaac Sim," as a simulation in 'Isaac Lab in Omniverse Digital Twin' shows numerous robots training on complex terrain with increasing iterations (500, 1000, 3000). A detailed diagram then outlines the data pipeline from 'NVIDIA Isaac Sim', text, video, and real robot data through 'NVIDIA OSMO' and heterogeneous compute to 'NVIDIA Project GROOT' and the humanoid robot, breaking down 'NVIDIA DGX Train' and 'NVIDIA OVX Simulate'. The narrator elaborates, "And we scale out with Osmo, a new compute orchestration service that coordinates workflows across DGX systems for training and OVX systems for simulation." The video then presents a split-screen comparison of a simulated and real kitchen environment, both featuring a teal robot performing tasks. The female voice concludes, "With these tools, we can train Groot in physically based simulation and transfer zero-shot to the real world," as a man wearing a VR headset is shown in a lab, interacting with a robot that places a mug on a table, with others observing in the background, and the music fades out.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a high-angle shot of a warehouse simulation labeled 'Omniverse Digital Twin' and 'Amazon Robotics,' showing blue robotic carts moving boxes through an automated facility. The scene transitions to a lab where a woman in a gray shirt interacts with a white humanoid robot, waving her hand as the robot mimics the gesture, with a large screen displaying a 3D simulation in the background. Next, a wide shot reveals a spacious lab with multiple humanoid robots—some teal and black, others white and gray—navigating an obstacle course while engineers observe. The focus shifts to a kitchen setting where a white humanoid robot with black gloves holds a laptop, labeled 'Apptronik,' and then to a woman in a teal shirt guiding a teal robot with a tablet, labeled 'Agility Robotics.' A diagram appears, illustrating 'NVIDIA Project GROOT' processing task prompts from language, videos, and demonstrations to generate actions for a humanoid robot, with feedback from observations. This is followed by a simulation in 'Isaac Lab in Omniverse Digital Twin,' showing numerous robots training on a complex terrain with increasing iterations (500, 1000, 3000). Another diagram details the data pipeline from NVIDIA Isaac Sim, text, video, and real robot data through NVIDIA OSMO and heterogeneous compute to Project GROOT and the humanoid robot, with a breakdown of NVIDIA DGX Train and OVX Simulate. The video then shows a split-screen comparison of a simulated and real kitchen environment, both featuring a teal robot performing tasks. Finally, a man wearing a VR headset is shown in a lab, interacting with a robot that places a mug on a table, while others observe in the background.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, futuristic instrumental track as a split-screen comparison shows a humanoid robot, labeled 'Teleoperated' and 'Autonomous,' pouring liquid in a kitchen, demonstrating its ability to handle everyday tasks. The music continues as the scene transitions to a Fourier Intelligence robot playing a red drum set, mirroring a human drummer, illustrating its capacity to emulate human movement just by observing us. A woman then performs a dance in front of a green wall, her movements tracked by a digital skeleton, which a humanoid robot on the right mimics in real-time. This is followed by a three-panel view showing the woman, an 'Isaac Lab in Omniverse Digital Twin' of the robot, and the physical robot, demonstrating seamless motion transfer and the training of models in simulation before deployment to physical robots. A technical diagram of the NVIDIA Project GROOT workflow appears, explaining how the GROOT model learns from human demonstrations and, when connected to a large language model, generates motions from natural language instructions. The scene shifts to a lab where a man in a white t-shirt observes a humanoid robot, identified as "G1" and "Dirt," as a digital twin on screen responds to his voice commands like "Hi, G1. Can you give me a high five?" and "Can you give us some cool moves?", with the robot executing these actions, including a "high five" and dynamic "cool moves" accompanied by distinct robot movement sounds. The narrator explains that all this incredible intelligence is powered by the new Jetson Thor Robotics chips, designed for GROOT and built for the future. The video concludes with two NVIDIA Jetson Thor Robotics chips with illuminated cores, followed by a teal and black humanoid robot walking outdoors on a bridge with a modern building in the background, showcasing its mobility and environmental navigation, as the narrator mentions Isaac Lab, Osmo, and GROOT providing the building blocks for such advancements.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment opens with a split-screen comparison of a humanoid robot performing a task in a kitchen, labeled 'Teleoperated' on the left and 'Autonomous' on the right, showcasing its ability to pour liquid from a bottle into a glass. The scene transitions to a demonstration of a robot playing a red drum set, mirroring a human drummer on the left, with the label 'Fourier Intelligence' indicating the technology provider. Next, a woman performs a dance move in front of a green wall, with her body movements tracked by a digital skeleton overlay, while a humanoid robot on the right mimics her actions in real-time. This is followed by a three-panel view: the woman on the left, a digital twin of the robot in the center labeled 'Isaac Lab in Omniverse Digital Twin,' and the physical robot on the right, demonstrating seamless motion transfer. A technical diagram then illustrates the NVIDIA Project GROOT workflow, where a user's voice is converted to text via NVIDIA Tokkio, processed by Project GROOT, and translated into robot actions with audio feedback. The scene shifts to a lab where a man in a white t-shirt observes a robot, while a digital twin on screen responds to voice commands like 'high-five' and 'cool moves,' with the robot executing the actions in real-time. The video then shows two NVIDIA chips with green and blue illuminated cores, followed by a humanoid robot walking outdoors on a bridge, demonstrating mobility and environmental navigation. The robot is shown from multiple angles, including a close-up of its head and torso, as it moves along a railing with a modern building in the background, emphasizing its dynamic capabilities and real-world integration.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, futuristic electronic score accompanying a dynamic outdoor scene featuring a futuristic industrial facility with solar panels and a sleek black car driving along a road. A female voiceover declares, "for the next generation of AI-powered robotics." The scene then transitions to a dark, minimalist stage where NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, walks confidently. His clear, confident voice with an American accent states, "The soul of NVIDIA. The intersection of computer graphics, physics, artificial intelligence. It all came to bear at this moment. The name of that project, General Robotics 003." He gestures while speaking, emphasizing his points, as he walks between two humanoid robots—one with a teal and white design and the other with a white and black design. He adds, "I know. Super good," eliciting light chuckles from the audience. The camera pulls back to reveal a full stage lined with eight humanoid robots of varying designs, standing in a row as he walks among them. Jensen then announces, "Well, I think we have some special guests. Do we?" as the audience erupts in cheers and applause, and the music shifts to a rhythmic, driving electronic beat with robotic whirring and walking sounds. The scene shifts to a dimly lit stage where two smaller, boxy robots with glowing yellow eyes and white and purple bodies walk in unison, their movements accompanied by the robotic sounds. They then join a group of larger humanoid robots, all raising their arms in a synchronized motion, as Jensen Huang exclaims, "Hey guys! Do I understand you guys are powered by Jetson? They're powered by Jetson. Little Jetson robotics computers inside. They learn to walk." The audience, visible in the foreground, is bathed in purple lighting, capturing the moment on their devices, while on-screen text in the top right corner reads 'MARCH 18, 2024', and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo is displayed in the bottom right corner throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dark stage illuminated by focused lighting, showcasing a lineup of humanoid robots of varying designs and colors. The date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are displayed. As the camera pans, NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black pants, and white sneakers, walks onto the stage, holding a small black remote. He gestures toward the robots, then moves to the right as two smaller, boxy robots with rectangular heads and glowing yellow eyes begin to walk toward him with smooth, coordinated movements, accompanied by distinct whirring and clicking mechanical sounds. Jensen Huang stands still, observing them, and introduces them, stating, "Ladies and gentlemen, this, this is Orange and this is the famous Green. They are the BDX robots of Disney. Amazing Disney Research." The robots continue to move closer, one of them turning its head to face him, demonstrating advanced mobility and interaction capabilities. Jensen Huang then urges them, "Come on, you guys, let's wrap up. Let's go," and directs them, "I said right here." As the robots emit more distinct chirping and squawking sounds, he playfully asks, "What are you saying?" and interprets their noises, "No, it's not time to eat. It's not time to eat. I'll give you a snack in a moment. Let me finish up real quick." He concludes by encouraging, "Come on, Green, hurry up," as the futuristic, high-tech atmosphere emphasizes the robots' design and functionality in the minimalist, dark environment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens on a dark stage where a small, boxy robot with glowing yellow eyes, a rectangular head, and two antennae, resembling a retro-futuristic design with a white and brown body, walks unsteadily. The NVIDIA CEO, Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, enters from stage left, playfully stating "Stop wasting time." He gestures toward the robot, then spreads his arms wide as if presenting it, continuing to speak and gesture while holding a small black remote. The robot continues its jerky walk in a small circle beside him. Huang announces "Five things. Five things. First, a new industrial revolution," explaining that "Every data center should be accelerated" and that "A trillion dollars worth of installed data centers will become modernized." He adds that "a new way of doing software has emerged, generative AI," which will create "new infrastructure, dedicated to doing one thing and one thing only... AI generators." As he speaks, the scene transitions to a large screen displaying a diagram titled 'A NEW INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION' with four white line-drawn icons: 'NEW INDUSTRY' ($100 T GENERATIVE AI ACCELERATED COMPUTING), 'BLACKWELL PLATFORM', 'NIMs', and 'OMNIVERSE AND ISAAC ROBOTICS'. Huang continues, "A new industrial revolution. Second, the computer of this revolution, the computer of this generation, generative AI, trillion parameters, Blackwell. Insane amounts of computers and computing." He then humorously interjects, "Third, I'm trying to concentrate," as he returns to center stage, continuing his presentation with expressive hand gestures. The stage remains dark with focused lighting on the speaker and robot, and the on-screen text 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are visible in the corners throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, standing on a dark, minimalist stage. A small, boxy robot with a white and brown body, glowing yellow eyes, a rectangular head with two antennae, and a jerky walking gait moves across the stage beside him. On-screen text in the top right corner reads 'MARCH 18, 2024', with a purple 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right. Jensen Huang speaks, stating, "Good job. Third. New computer, new computer creates new types of software. New type of software should be distributed in a new way," gesturing expressively and occasionally pointing toward the robot or the audience. He continues, explaining how this software allows users to "take it with you. Because it is your intelligence. Your intelligence should be packaged up in a way that allows you to take it with you. We call them NIMs." The camera zooms in on his animated hand movements and facial expressions as he adds, "And third, these NIMs are going to help you create a new type of application for the future... you're going to integrate them like teams. Create these applications." He then gestures toward a large screen behind him, which displays a diagram titled 'A NEW INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION' with components including 'NIMs', 'NEMO AND NVIDIA AI FOUNDRY', and 'OMNIVERSE AND ISAAC ROBOTICS'. He elaborates, "We have a fantastic capability between NIMs, the AI technology, the tools, Nemo, and the infrastructure DGX Cloud, in our AI Foundry to help you create proprietary applications, proprietary chatbots." The robot continues to move beside him, occasionally turning its head. The camera zooms out to a wide shot, showing Jensen Huang and the robot on stage with the full diagram visible behind them. He concludes, "And then lastly, everything that moves in the future will be robotic. You're not going to be the only one. And these robotic systems, whether they..." as the robot briefly turns and walks toward the center of the stage before stopping. The segment ends with Jensen Huang continuing to speak, gesturing toward the screen and the robot, emphasizing the integration of AI, robotics, and computing platforms in a new industrial era.

segments.integrated_caption: NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black leather jacket, stands on a dark, minimalist stage, gesturing expressively as he speaks. A small, boxy robot with glowing yellow eyes and a jerky gait moves beside him. On-screen text in the top right corner reads 'MARCH 18, 2024', with a purple 'y/ highlights' logo in the bottom right. Huang's clear voice explains the need for a digital platform for 'humanoid, AMRs, self-driving cars, forklifts, manipulating arms,' and 'robotic factories,' identifying it as 'Omniverse, the operating system of the robotics world.' The camera zooms in on Huang, then transitions to a wide shot revealing a large screen behind him displaying a technical diagram titled 'A NEW INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION,' detailing components like 'BLACKWELL PLATFORM' and 'OMNIVERSE AND ISAAC ROBOTICS.' Huang refers to 'the five things that we talked about today' and then asks, 'What does NVIDIA look like?' The scene cuts to a detailed graphic of the 'NVIDIA Blackwell Platform' hardware components. Huang emphatically declares, 'This is what we announced to you today. This is Blackwell. This is the platform.' The audience, seated in a dimly lit auditorium bathed in purple lighting, is shown attentively watching, and erupts in enthusiastic applause following Huang's announcement. The scene returns to Huang on stage with the robot, gesturing toward the screen as the applause continues.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang standing on a dark, minimalist stage, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote. He gestures expressively as he speaks, with a small, boxy robot with a white and brown body, glowing yellow eyes, and a jerky walking gait moving beside him. The stage is lit with focused spotlights, creating high contrast against the black background. On-screen text in the top right corner reads 'MARCH 18, 2024', and a purple 'y/ highlights' logo is in the bottom right. As Huang continues his presentation, the camera zooms in on him, capturing his animated hand movements and facial expressions. The scene transitions to a wide shot revealing a large screen behind him displaying a technical diagram titled 'A NEW INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION', which includes five components: 'NEW INDUSTRY' (with $100T, Generative AI, Accelerated Computing), 'BLACKWELL PLATFORM', 'NIMs', 'NEMO AND NVIDIA AI FOUNDRY', and 'OMNIVERSE AND ISAAC ROBOTICS'. The camera then cuts to a detailed graphic of the 'NVIDIA Blackwell Platform', showcasing various hardware components including the HGX B100, NVLink Switch, GB200 Superchip Compute Node, Quantum X800 Switch, ConnectX-8 SuperNIC, and Spectrum X800 Switch BlueField-3 SuperNIC. The audience, visible in a wide shot, is seated in a dimly lit auditorium bathed in purple lighting, attentively watching the presentation. The scene returns to Huang on stage, now with the boxy robot still moving beside him, as he gestures toward the screen, emphasizing the platform's capabilities.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang, dressed in a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote on a dark, minimalist stage. He speaks with enthusiasm, detailing "amazing, amazing processors, NVLink switches, networking systems," and declares, "the system design is a miracle. This is Blackwell. This to me is what a GPU looks like in my mind." The audience responds with loud, sustained applause. Jensen then asks, "I think we have one more treat for everybody. What do you think, should we?" As he speaks, a small, boxy robot with a white and brown body, glowing yellow eyes, and antennae moves across the stage with a jerky gait, emitting distinct mechanical whirring and clanking sounds. Jensen exclaims, "Okay. We have one more thing to show you. Roll it," as the robot continues its mechanical movements. The screen behind him briefly displayed 'Spectrum X800 Switch', 'BlueField-3 SuperNIC', and 'ConnectX-8 SuperNIC'. The scene transitions to a large, detailed graphic of the 'NVIDIA Blackwell Platform', showcasing various hardware components like the HGX B100 and GB200 Superchip Compute Node, with Jensen small in the foreground emphasizing its scale. A dramatic, futuristic orchestral score begins, accompanied by sci-fi sound effects, as the scene cuts to an animated sequence. A sleek, dark spaceship navigates a vibrant purple and blue nebula. Inside, a hand types 'Show me NVIDIA technologies' into a futuristic interface, which responds with 'NVIDIA Onboard Assistant' and prompts to 'initiate NeVA auto pilot', accompanied by interface beeps and typing sounds. The spaceship then accelerates, its engines glowing orange with a powerful whoosh, flying through the cosmic environment. Throughout, the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are visible.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang on a dark, minimalist stage, wearing a black textured leather jacket, black shirt, black pants, and white sneakers, holding a small black remote. He gestures toward a small, boxy robot with a white and brown body, glowing yellow eyes, a rectangular head with two antennae, and a jerky walking gait, which moves across the stage. The screen behind him displays text listing 'Spectrum X800 Switch', 'BlueField-3 SuperNIC', and 'ConnectX-8 SuperNIC'. He then speaks directly to the audience, using expressive hand gestures. The scene transitions to a large, detailed graphic of the 'NVIDIA Blackwell Platform', showcasing various hardware components: the HGX B100, NVLink Switch, GB200 Superchip Compute Node, Quantum X800 Switch, ConnectX-8 SuperNIC, and Spectrum X800 Switch BlueField-3 SuperNIC, all arranged around a central rack system. Jensen Huang stands small in the foreground, emphasizing the scale of the platform. The scene then cuts to a futuristic, animated sequence of a sleek, dark spaceship navigating through a vibrant nebula of purple and blue clouds. Inside the ship, a hand types 'Show me NVIDIA technologies' into a futuristic interface, which responds with 'NVIDIA Onboard Assistant' and a prompt to 'initiate NeVA auto pilot'. The spaceship then accelerates, its engines glowing orange, as it flies through the cosmic environment. Throughout, the date 'MARCH 18, 2024' and the 'y/ highlights' logo are visible in the corners.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with intense, futuristic electronic music accompanying a sleek, dark spaceship with glowing orange engines as it navigates a blue, bubble-filled environment, suggesting an underwater or digital realm. The music builds tension before shifting to a more triumphant, anthemic orchestral piece as the scene moves to a large, dark auditorium where a massive screen displays a cosmic nebula with the text 'Powering the New Era of Computing', followed by the NVIDIA logo. He stands beside a display of three open server modules labeled 'H100' and 'H200', gesturing as he begins his presentation, his clear and enthusiastic voice, with a slight accent, heard saying "Thank you!", "Thank you, have a great, have a great GTC!", and "Thank you all for coming!".
